The raven locks that once were rich and deep,
Are streaked with silver now, and thin they grow;
The eyes that shone, now heavy seem with sleep,
And youth's bright fire begins to burn full low.
For Time, that thief, steals beauty's golden hour,
And plants his furrows where the roses played,
He nips the bud, and doth consume the flower,
Leaving but ghosts of what his hand hath made.
Alas, no remedy can stay his hand,
No magic charm can make his scythe delay,
He sweeps all life throughout the weary land,
And bears our fleeting glories far away.
Yet in these lines, some shadow may remain,
To tell the world thy beauty lived in vain.


Shall I compare thy wit to diamonds bright,
Whose keenest facets pierce the mind's dark night?
Or to the sun, that brings the world to light,
And doth disperse all shadows with its might?
No, for thy thoughts are swifter than the day,
More subtle than the moon's ethereal gleam,
They dance like fairies in a sportive way,
And wake the slumbering soul from drowsy dream.
Thy words, like honey, flow with such delight,
They charm the ear, and soothe the troubled breast,
Dispelling sorrow with their gentle might,
And granting comfort to the sore distressed.
Thus doth thy mind a wondrous kingdom hold,
More rich than gems, more precious far than gold.


When absent from the solace of thine eyes,
My heart, a rudderless and fragile bark,
Is tossed on waves of sorrow, sighs on sighs,
And wanders blindly through the bitter dark.
The sun itself seems to withdraw its gleam,
The birds forget their joyous morning lay,
And life itself appears a troubled dream,
Since thou, my guiding star, art far away.
Each moment stretched, an age of endless pain,
Each thought a thorn, each memory a sting,
I long to feel thy tender hand again,
And hear the music that thy voice doth bring.
Come back, my love, and banish all my fear,
And let thy presence make my spirit clear.


Sleep, gentle Sleep, thou balm of weary minds,
That knitt'st up sorrows, and dost ease all pain,
Thou art a comfort that the troubled finds,
And bring'st oblivion to life's harsh domain.
When troubled thoughts like spectres haunt the day,
And reason's lamp burns low with anxious dread,
Thou com'st with silence, chasing cares away,
And lay'st a soft hand on the aching head.
Thy drowsy touch can calm the fevered breast,
And bid the restless spirit find its rest.
In thy soft arms, all mortals are made blest,
From king to peasant, by thy grace caressed.
So welcome, Sleep, descend with silent wing,
And to my soul thy sweet repose now bring.


The fickle world, whose praises ebb and flow,
Doth lift its darlings high, then casts them down;
And fortune's wheel, that doth so swiftly go,
Bestows a laurel, then withdraws a crown.
Who trusts her smiles, builds castles in the air,
For her false promises betray the wise,
She lures with hopes, then plunges to despair,
And mocks the tears that stream from weeping eyes.
Let others chase her gaudy, painted prize,
And stake their peace upon her shifting sands,
My soul shall seek where truer comfort lies,
Beyond the grasp of her capricious hands.
For lasting joy, no earthly wealth can buy,
It dwells within, where truest virtues lie.


My friend, whose counsel lights my darkest way,
And whose firm hand sustains my faltering tread,
What words can tell the debt I owe this day,
For comfort granted, and for solace spread?
When worldly cares did press upon my soul,
And bitter envy poisoned every thought,
Thy gentle wisdom made my spirit whole,
And peace, which I had lost, was newly caught.
No fleeting fancy ties my heart to thine,
But bonds of truth, by trial made more strong,
A sacred trust, a loyalty divine,
That shall endure though ages roll along.
So long as life within this breast shall dwell,
My grateful heart thy goodness shall compel.


When Nature's art doth paint the eastern skies,
With hues of crimson, gold, and tender blue,
And morning's breath bids drowsy creatures rise,
Unveiling all the world to mortal view;
I marvel at the glory of the light,
The dew-kissed grass, the blossoms freshly born,
Dispelling shadows of the vanished night,
And ushering in a sweet and hopeful morn.
Each tiny leaf, a miracle to see,
Each feathered songster, pouring forth its strain,
Reveals a power, boundless, wild, and free,
That banishes all sorrow, grief, and pain.
Oh, that my soul could hold this joy complete,
And learn from earth a lesson pure and sweet!


Thou art not fair, for beauty's fleeting show,
Can oft deceive, and lead the heart astray;
Nor wise, for wisdom sometimes doth not know
The hidden snares that lurk along life's way.
But thou art true, a virtue rarely found,
In this false age, where honesty grows cold;
Thy heart sincere, on firmest honour ground,
More precious far than gems or purest gold.
Let outward grace with changing fashion turn,
And painted charms allure the common eye,
From thy fixed spirit I desire to learn,
How steadfast faith can make a soul stand high.
So hold thy truth, let others praise the fair,
For in thy truth, a lasting beauty's there.


When first I saw thy face, it was as dawn,
Breaking upon a world of weary night,
And all my sorrow, then, was swiftly gone,
Absorbed and vanquished by thy radiant light.
Thy smile, a sunbeam, chased away the fear,
Thy gentle voice, a melody so sweet,
It filled my spirit, banished every tear,
And made my trembling heart with gladness beat.
I knew not love could hold such potent sway,
To mend a soul that sorrow had undone,
And lead me from the paths of dark dismay,
Into a haven, where new hopes had spun.
So blest am I, that fate did thee impart,
To be the sovereign ruler of my heart.


How many woes doth man endure below,
From false ambition, and from love betrayed!
What bitter fruits from seeds of malice grow,
By envious tongues and broken vows displayed!
The world's a stage, where fickle actors play,
Their tragic parts, with masks of painted lies,
And swiftly fade the glory of a day,
Beneath the gaze of unforgiving skies.
O, were there strength to pierce the veiled deceit,
And read the heart that hides its dark design,
To crush the serpent 'neath our righteous feet,
And bring to light the treachery malign!
But truth must wait till time's last curtain falls,
When righteous judgment answers virtue's calls.


When winter winds do howl and skies are grey,
And all the verdant leaves have turned to brown,
My memory flies to one far summer's day,
When love's first blossom opened in this town.
I see again the sunlight on thy hair,
Thy laughing eyes, that held the heaven's blue,
The sweet communion, banishing all care,
And whispers soft, that promised always true.
Though seasons change, and years do swiftly roll,
And time's cold hand doth touch with chill regret,
That golden image lives within my soul,
A cherished joy, I cannot quite forget.
So in my heart, that summer lingers still,
Defying winter's unrelenting will.


The bitter draught of sorrow I must drain,
For parting's hour doth bring a heavy toll;
And absence looms, a source of endless pain,
To tear apart the fabric of my soul.
No sun will shine as brightly as before,
No song of birds will bring such sweet delight,
For half my being journeys to a shore,
Where shadows lengthen, and where fades the light.
Oh, that our days were linked in endless chain,
That fate might never force us far apart,
To suffer this intolerable pain,
And bear the rending of a broken heart.
Yet in my thoughts, thou ever shalt reside,
And be my comfort, though we walk not side by side.


The earth, with greedy mouth, consumes its own,
And Time, that tyrant, spares no living thing,
All flesh returns to dust, and overthrown,
Are kings and paupers, by his sweeping wing.
Yet man, with brief and fragile span of breath,
Doth strive to leave some token of his stay,
Against the final victory of death,
A verse, a deed, to shine beyond his day.
For love and truth, though mortal life may cease,
Are etched in hearts, beyond decay's control,
They find in noble minds a lasting peace,
And live forever in the deathless soul.
So fear not dissolution's dark command,
For truth and love shall ever firmly stand.


When cruel time doth steal fair beauty's hold,
And youthful prime from fading cheek doth fly,
What comfort then shall make our story told,
Or shield us from oblivion's heavy eye?
For earthly glory, with a swift advance,
Doth wither swift, as summer's tender rose,
And all our fleeting joy, a fading trance,
Into the silent dust and darkness goes.
But if thy spirit in these lines doth dwell,
Though age and pain thy outward grace consume,
My humble verse thy wondrous worth shall tell,
And snatch thy memory from time's cold tomb.
So shalt thou live, beyond thy mortal stay,
And conquer all, save death's appointed day.


My love, a sun that knows no dark eclipse,
Doth shine as constant as the northern star,
No fickle breeze, nor treachery of lips,
Can taint the truth of what our spirits are.
Though seasons change and flowers lose their hue,
And swift decay makes strong foundations fall,
My steadfast heart shall ever rest with you,
And answer to your spirit's gentle call.
Let fortune frown, or envious tongues conspire,
To cast a shadow where our joys have grown,
My soul shall burn with an unyielding fire,
And find its solace in your love alone.
For true affection mocks at worldly strife,
And proves the very anchor of my life.


Shall I compare thy grace to blossoms frail,
Whose tender beauty fades at morning's dew?
Or to the moon, whose silver light doth fail
When harsh clouds gather, hiding her from view?
No, for thy virtues shine with brighter gleam,
More constant than the sun in summer's heat,
Dispelling all the shadows of a dream,
And making every bitter moment sweet.
No envious frost can touch thy gentle heart,
Nor winter's tempest dim thy inner light,
For nature did but lend thee her best art,
To banish sorrow, and adorn the night.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


When troubled thoughts within my bosom dwell,
And fortune's adverse glance doth make me sigh,
I turn to thee, my comfort and my well,
And find the solace which I cannot buy.
For in thy gaze, a gentle peace I find,
A balm to heal the wounds of worldly care,
A sanctuary for my wearied mind,
Escaping from each burden I must bear.
The envious crowd may mock my simple plea,
And deem my passion but a fool's desire,
But little know they what thou art to me,
Thou kindler of my soul's undying fire.
So let them scorn, their judgments I defy,
For in thy love, I choose to live and die.


The fleeting hours, that bear us to our end,
Do steal our youth and beauty day by day,
And swift as shadows, time doth often bend
Our firmest purpose, bearing it away.
The eye that sparkled with a youthful gleam,
The cheek where roses freshly did reside,
Shall yield their glory to oblivion's stream,
And be by wrinkled age and sorrow tried.
Yet fear not, love, though years conspire to chill
Thy outward grace, and steal thy youthful prime,
My constant verse shall keep thy beauty still,
And mock the ravage of relentless time.
For in these lines, thy memory shall stay,
And live forever, till the judgment day.


How oft I wander, lost in silent thought,
And search for comfort that I cannot find,
For every joy with bitter sorrow's fraught,
And leaves a restless torment in my mind.
The world, a stage where fleeting shadows glide,
Doth offer little for a soul like mine,
But masks and feigning, where no truths abide,
And hollow praises, meant but to decline.
Then do I turn to beauty's gentle face,
And find a refuge from the common strife,
A vision fair, replete with truth and grace,
That brings sweet purpose to my weary life.
So in thy presence, all my sorrows cease,
And my vexed spirit finds enduring peace.


My restless heart, by cares of life oppressed,
Doth yearn for quiet, far from worldly sound,
To lay its burdens on a tender breast,
Where truest sympathy may still be found.
For common converse yields but shallow praise,
And hollow laughter hides a secret sting,
While false companions tread divergent ways,
And every pleasure doth its sorrow bring.
But when thy image rises to my view,
A sudden light doth pierce the gathering gloom,
My weary spirit feels its strength renew,
And hope springs forth, like flowers from a tomb.
So in thy love, my true contentment lies,
And finds its heaven mirrored in thine eyes.


The envious hours, with stealthy, silent tread,
Do steal the freshness from the morning's light,
And bring the shadows where fair youth was bred,
Consuming all that blossoms pure and bright.
The lily's whiteness and the rose's flush,
The dimpled cheek, the laughter in the eye,
Are hurried onward in a silent rush,
To perish with a soft and fading sigh.
Yet shall my verse, if truth it can impart,
Preserve the essence of thy fairer claim,
And plant thy beauty deep within each heart,
Immortalized within this deathless frame.
So shalt thou live, beyond all earthly scope,
A lasting monument to love and hope.


My muse, though oft inclined to deeper themes,
Of Time's decay, or Fortune's cruel deceit,
Is drawn by thee, as rivers to their streams,
To sing thy praises, wondrous, pure, and sweet.
For in thy form, such perfect grace doth lie,
As nature did in lavish bounty mould,
To charm the heart and captivate the eye,
A priceless treasure, worth far more than gold.
And though my skill but lends a feeble aid,
To paint the splendor which I scarce define,
Let truth itself within these lines be laid,
To show the world a beauty so divine.
So read these verses, though they falter oft,
And know my love, that lifts my spirit aloft.


The fleeting breath that gives us life and fire,
Doth swiftly pass, as shadows on the wall,
And all our earthly hopes, our fond desire,
Are destined soon to vanish, fade, and fall.
The strongest oak, by time is brought to dust,
The proudest towers crumble to the ground,
And all we cherish, built on mortal trust,
By ruin's hand, too quickly is unbound.
But fear not, friend, though death may claim its toll,
And steal the vigour from thy youthful years,
There is a part of thee, a deathless soul,
That conquers sorrow and transcends all fears.
So let thy spirit rise above decay,
And live forever, past thy fleeting day.


O, let me not compare thee to a flower,
Whose fragile petals wither in the sun,
Or to a joy that lasts but for an hour,
Before its brief, sweet pilgrimage is run.
For in thy spirit, true perfections dwell,
A steadfast light that knows no slow decline,
A spring of wisdom, from a hidden well,
And virtues pure, that make thy essence shine.
Let envious age attempt to steal thy grace,
And furrow lines upon thy youthful brow,
My verse shall hold thy beauty in this place,
And speak thy praises, as I speak them now.
So shall thy worth forever be renowned,
When fleeting glories lie beneath the ground.


My weary eyes, by day with tasks oppressed,
Do long for slumber, bringing sweet release,
To lay my thoughts upon a tranquil rest,
And find within the night a silent peace.
But then thy image steals into my mind,
And banishes all comfort from my bed,
For every sense to thee is then inclined,
And restless visions fill my waking head.
My spirit flies to thee on airy wings,
And haunts thy presence through the lonely night,
Imagining the joy thy presence brings,
And feasting on thy beauty's gentle light.
Thus, though by day I toil, by night I weep,
For thee I wake, for thee I cannot sleep.


When sorrow's cloud doth dim my spirit's sight,
And worldly cares with heavy burdens press,
I call on thee, my beacon and my light,
To ease the weight of my profound distress.
For in thy gentle mien, a balm I find,
To soothe the anguish of a troubled breast,
A sacred haven for my weary mind,
Where all my anxious thoughts are laid to rest.
Let others seek for fame or worldly power,
And chase the shadows of a fleeting dream,
My heart finds solace in that peaceful hour,
When thy bright image doth upon me beam.
So in thy love, my truest joy I see,
And all my comfort is derived from thee.


My pen, though weak, doth strive with earnest might,
To capture beauty ere it pass away,
And bring thy virtues to eternal light,
To shine forever, past thy mortal day.
For all too swift do earthly glories fade,
And bloom and perish in a single hour,
As by the hand of cruel oblivion made,
To yield their freshness to destruction's power.
But what I write, though simple it may seem,
Shall bear thy image to a distant age,
And prove thy worth, no idle, fading dream,
But truth enshrined upon this humble page.
So shall thy praise through future years be known,
When monuments of stone are overthrown.


When first thy gentle gaze did meet mine eye,
A sudden warmth did through my bosom spread,
As winter's ice beneath the spring suns lie,
And dormant spirits from their slumber shed.
No greater beauty hath the world revealed,
Than in the depths of thy celestial sight,
My every doubt and fear were then repealed,
And all my being bathed in purest light.
For in those pools, where love itself doth dwell,
I see a heaven far beyond the stars,
A sacred truth, a magic potent spell,
That heals all wounds and mends all ancient scars.
O, let me gaze, and drown within that sea,
And hold my soul forever bound to thee.


My heart, a prisoner, bound by silken chain,
Doth pine and languish for thy cold regard,
And though I plead with fervent prayer in vain,
Thy stony will doth ever stand on guard.
I pour my passion forth, a weeping stream,
That seeks to melt the frost upon thy breast,
But all my labours fade as any dream,
And leave my spirit grievously oppressed.
Why dost thou scorn the love that I do bear?
Or deem my constant troth of little worth?
A crueler fate no mortal soul can share,
Than to be spurned by her who gave him birth
To sorrow's thrall, and grief's perpetual sting,
While hope takes flight on disappointment's wing.


When April's breath doth kiss the sleeping earth,
And tender buds begin their shy display,
A joyous spirit springs from nature's birth,
And birds sing sweetly at the break of day.
The meadows don their robes of emerald green,
The sunbeams dance upon the crystal stream,
A fairer sight hath seldom e'er been seen,
As if the world were waking from a dream.
Yet all this beauty, though it doth abound,
And tempt the eye with colours bright and bold,
Is but a shadow on enchanted ground,
Compared to charms thy lovely form doth hold.
For nature's art, however fair and grand,
Is but a foil for beauty in thy hand.


How swiftly doth a smile upon thy face,
Dissolve the clouds that gather in my mind,
And bring to being such a joyful grace,
That all my previous sorrows are defined
As naught but shadows, fleeting and unsure,
That vanish quite when thou dost deign to beam,
A sacred magic, potent to allure,
More bright than any poet's fairest dream.
For in that light, a thousand flowers bloom,
And birds burst forth in melody so sweet,
Dispelling every hint of winter's gloom,
Making my trembling heart with gladness beat.
O, let thy smile forever be my guide,
And in its warmth, my willing soul abide.


When thoughtful Memory doth present to mind
The days of old, when youth and joy were free,
A bitter sweetness doth my spirit find,
Recalling moments spent, dear friend, with thee.
For though the pleasure of those times was great,
And laughter echoed through the sunlit hours,
I know too well that unrelenting fate
Hath plucked and scattered all those tender flowers.
The vanished past, a ghost that haunts my sleep,
Doth bring both comfort and a gentle pain,
For joys recalled make weary spirits weep,
To know they shall not e'er return again.
Yet hold I dear each moment we have known,
Though memory's gift be mixed with sorrow's moan.


Ah, where are now the days of childhood's glee,
When every morn brought wonders to explore?
And burdens light, and spirits wild and free,
Ere knowledge opened sorrow's bitter door?
The world then seemed a garden, fresh and green,
Where evil's shadow dared not cast its stain,
And simple truths were readily foreseen,
Before life's tempest brought its chilling rain.
Now wisdom's heavy hand doth press me low,
And worldly cares do weigh upon my soul,
The paths I tread, with thorns do thickly grow,
And broken dreams extract their heavy toll.
Oh, for a heart untainted, pure, and bright,
To walk again in childhood's golden light.


The world doth hold a mirror to the eye,
And often shows a face both false and fair,
Where painted smiles a hidden malice lie,
And outward show doth mask a foul despair.
The gaudy flower that boasts its splendid hue,
May yet contain a poison deep within,
And spoken vows, though seeming ever true,
May hide the hollow heart of deepest sin.
But thou, my love, art fashioned without art,
Thy virtues shine, no borrowed light they claim,
A truth profound resides within thy heart,
Unstained by falsehood, pure as any flame.
So let the world its fleeting masks embrace,
Thy genuine worth outshines all outward grace.


When centuries have crumbled towers of might,
And ancient cities turn to dust and sand,
And Time, with hungry maw, devours the light,
That once illumed the proudest of the land,
Thy beauty shall not yield to such decay,
Nor fade away with mortal, fleeting breath,
But live in these poor lines, from day to day,
Triumphant over ruin, age, and death.
For in this scroll, thy image shall remain,
More lasting than the carven stone or brass,
Escaping Time's inexorable chain,
As generations come and swiftly pass.
So read thy name, embalmed in poet's art,
A deathless wonder, fixed within my heart.


How oft I gaze upon thy lovely face,
And find no answer to my silent plea,
No gentle warmth, no sympathetic grace,
But cold indifference, ever turned to me.
My faithful heart, though brimming full of love,
Doth beat in vain against a wall of stone,
While hope's faint star is banished far above,
And I am left to suffer here alone.
I crave no riches, nor a crown to wear,
No worldly honours, nor a great renown,
But just one kindly glance, one word to share,
To ease the sorrow that hath borne me down.
Alas, dear love, this pain I must endure,
A heart unloved finds no enduring cure.


My love for thee, a pilgrim's steady quest,
Doth walk through sun and storm, through thorn and mire,
Nor seeks a pause, nor craves a moment's rest,
But onward presses with unflagging fire.
Through deserts bare, where solace finds no stay,
And mountains steep, where trials do abound,
My constant spirit finds its destined way,
And yields no ground, though dangers hem it round.
The path is long, and shadows oft descend,
And weariness doth sometimes claim its toll,
But at each step, my thoughts to thee I send,
As to the sacred altar of my soul.
So let me journey, till my life is done,
My pilgrimage of love, forever run.


When forty winters shall besiege thy brow,
And make deep trenches where fair beauty lay,
Thy youth's proud livery, so gazèd on now,
Will be but tattered rags, consumed by day.
Then being asked where all thy beauty lies,
Where all the treasure of thy lusty prime,
To say within thine own deep-sunken eyes,
Were an all-eating shame, and thriftless crime.
How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use,
If thou couldst answer, "This fair child of mine
Shall sum my count, and make my old excuse,"
Proving thy beauty's worth, and making thine.
This were to be new born when thou art old,
And see thy blood warm when thou feel'st it cold.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


The glorious lamp of heaven, the sun,
Doth not disdain to shine upon the stream,
Nor doth the ocean scorn to turn and run
From mighty banks to kiss the earth's soft dream.
Even so, my love, a greater light than these,
A purer flame doth in thy spirit dwell,
That warms my heart and puts my soul at ease,
And banishes the power of any spell.
For in thine eyes I see a gentle ray
That speaks of kindness and a noble mind,
And in thy smile there dawns a brighter day
That leaves all earthly troubles far behind.
So let no cloud obscure this beauteous sight,
But shine, my love, with ever constant light.


Why is my verse so barren of new pride,
So far from variation or quick change?
Why with the time do I not glance aside
To new-found methods and to compounds strange?
Why write I still all one, ever the same,
And keep invention in a noted weed,
That every word doth almost tell my name,
Showing their birth and where they did proceed?
O, know, sweet love, I always write of you,
And you alone fill every line I pen,
And what is new is but what is most true,
For in your beauty is my freshest ken.
So all my poems to one subject cleave,
And what is yours, in them, I strive to leave.


When cruel Time, with his swift-footed pace,
Shall spoil the beauty of thy youthful prime,
And furrowed wrinkles mar thy lovely face,
Consuming all that's fair within his time,
Then shall the spirit of my loving verse
Preserve thy virtues from oblivion's dust,
And like a beacon, brilliantly disperse
The shadows cast by Rust's corroding rust.
Though lofty towers no more erect shall stand,
And marble effigies resign their breath,
Thy worth, upheld by this poetic hand,
Shall triumph o'er the silent pangs of death.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


When I behold the world's most fading show,
And see how riches melt and honours waste,
How fickle fortune makes men high and low,
And what swift ruin follows envious haste;
Then think I on thy firm and constant heart,
Whose truth no change of season can confound,
A jewel set from transient things apart,
On virtue's rock, immovably firm bound.
Let others chase the shadow of a name,
Or bend their knees to power's uncertain sway;
My truest treasure is thy loyal flame,
That shines unwavering through the darkest day.
What earthly goods compare to thee, my friend?
Thou art my guide, until my journey's end.


Mine eye hath made a league with my sad heart,
To see itself in every lovely show;
For what the eye beholds, it doth impart
Unto the heart, where thoughts of sorrow grow.
But when I gaze on thy most gracious face,
A sudden balm doth all my pains assuage;
Then banish'd is all grief from sorrow's place,
And joy doth hold my troubled soul in cage.
Thus do I find in thee my perfect cure,
A sovereign medicine for each dire ill;
Whose beauty makes my heavy spirit pure,
And bids my stormy passions to be still.
So let me ever on thy features dwell,
For in thy sight, my heart is ever well.


Why should my verse be barren of all praise,
When every other pen doth gild thy name?
Why do I stand mute in these latter days,
While others sing thy worth with burning flame?
It is not that my love doth less abound,
But that I fear to stain thy perfect grace,
By weaving words that are not truly sound,
Or fail to capture beauty of thy face.
For when I strive to tell what in thee lies,
My clumsy tongue doth falter and doth cease;
I find no metaphor beneath the skies
To match thy worth, and bring my soul to peace.
So let my silence speak my deeper heart,
More eloquent than any printed art.


O, how much more doth beauty beauteous seem
By that sweet ornament which truth doth give!
The rose looks fairer, when we it esteem
Not for its scent alone, but that it live.
The canker blooms have thorns, and summer's breath
Smells far more sweet, though tainted by the weed,
But virtue lives beyond decay and death,
If in the soul, a loyal truth take seed.
The fairest flower that blossoms in the field,
If it but perish, unregarded dies;
But if sweet virtue do its perfume yield,
It triumphs over time in deathless guise.
So thee, my dearest, virtue doth adorn,
And makes thy loveliness outshine the morn.


When I behold the stars, which clearly shine,
And mark their motion in the vaulted sky,
How they revolve by certain laws divine,
And through the boundless firmament do fly;
Then do I muse upon our earthly state,
And how our lives, like shadows, pass away,
Subject to fortune's ever-changing gate,
That gives us joy, then takes it on a day.
For what is man, that he should hope to stand
Against the currents of devouring time?
A fragile vessel, built of shifting sand,
That sinks at last, forgotten in its prime.
But love, which dwells within the constant heart,
Shall play no transient, but eternal part.


My thoughts, like weary travellers, do stray
To thee, whose image fills my lonely mind;
And finding no repose throughout the day,
Return at night, no joyful solace find.
For thou art gone, and with thee all my light,
My summer's warmth, my comfort and my cheer;
And I am left to dwell in endless night,
While every moment brings a starting tear.
Oh, absence, foe to every loving soul!
That dost torment me with such cruel despite,
Thou mak'st my spirit lose all self-control,
And banishest all colour from my sight.
So haste thee home, my sun, my dearest friend,
And let this weary separation end.


The weary world, oppressed by age and care,
Doth often sigh for pleasures that are fled;
And all the burden that its creatures bear
Seems but a prelude to the silent dead.
For beauty fades, and strength doth soon decay,
And wisdom's light grows dim with passing years;
The fondest hopes, like flowers, pass away,
And joy itself dissolves in sudden tears.
But when I think on thee, my dearest love,
A sudden hope within my spirit springs;
As if a message from the heavens above
Had brought me tidings on an angel's wings.
For in thy grace, a timeless charm I find,
That soothes my soul and elevates my mind.


What can I give, to make thy beauty last,
When summer's gold shall turn to winter's grey?
And all thy grace, by cruel seasons cast,
Be swept by time, and withered quite away?
No painter's brush, nor poet's gilded pen,
Can catch the shadow of thy fleeting grace;
For art itself, though praised by mortal men,
Doth strive in vain to hold thee in thy place.
I see thee now, a rose within its bloom,
A transient wonder, fading by degrees;
And ponder deep upon thy coming doom,
Which steals thy glory, borne upon the breeze.
My love alone, eternal, shall not die,
But watch thy passing with a weeping eye.


The morning star doth herald forth the day,
And chase the lingering shadows from the sky;
So from my heart doth sorrow flee away,
When thy bright presence meets my longing eye.
For like a sun, thy beams do light my soul,
Dispelling clouds that hover o'er my mind;
And make my fractured spirit once more whole,
Leaving no trace of sadness there behind.
What bliss is this, that thou dost so impart,
A balm to grief, a solace to despair?
Thou art the sovereign medicine for my heart,
Beyond compare, a treasure rich and rare.
So let me bask within thy gentle ray,
And let my darkness yield to thy sweet day.


Though absence be a bitter, cruel behest,
To tear apart what love hath closely bound;
And leave the heart with disquiet and unrest,
Where only echoes of past joys are found.
Yet in this void, where shadows seem to cling,
Thy memory shines, a beacon in the night;
And to my soul, sweet consolation bring,
A living presence, banishing all fright.
For true affection knows no bounds of space,
Nor fears the distance that divides us now;
But holds thy image in its sacred place,
A silent promise, sealed by love's own vow.
So though we part, and distant paths we tread,
My heart remains where thy dear form is bred.


When fickle Fortune frowns upon my state,
And envious rumour doth my name defame;
When friends forsake me to a cruel fate,
And mock my sorrow, adding to my shame.
Then do I turn, with a despairing eye,
To recollect the days of brighter cheer;
And wish for death, to lay me down and die,
And end the burden of each passing year.
But then I think upon thy gentle grace,
Thy constant heart, that never will deceive;
And straight my spirit seeks its rightful place,
And learns anew to hope, and to believe.
For in thy love, my riches I behold,
Worth more than silver, or than purest gold.


Full many a time I marvel at the power
Of cruel deceit, that lurks beneath fair show;
How smooth-tongued treachery, in an evil hour,
Can cause the seeds of bitter hate to grow.
For friendships forged in youth, and sealed by trust,
Are often rent by malice and by spite;
And loyal hearts are trampled into dust,
Extinguished quite, like torches in the night.
O, what a wound, when those we held most dear,
Do turn their faces, and become our foe!
It poisons life, and fills the soul with fear,
And plants within a seed of endless woe.
So let me guard my heart from outward pride,
And trust in few, where constant truths abide.


The fleeting breath, that animates our clay,
Is but a loan, from nature's boundless store;
Which she demands, at some appointed day,
To mingle with the dust, and be no more.
What then of beauty, youth, and all that's fair?
Shall they descend to earth, and perish quite?
And leave no trace, no memory in the air,
But sink forgotten into endless night?
Ah no! For reason doth our minds engage,
To seek a solace 'gainst this certain doom;
And find in art, a timeless, sacred stage,
Where fading glories can regain their bloom.
So live, my verse, when I am laid to rest,
And bear her praises, living, and blessed.


The weary sun, when he hath run his course,
And drawn his golden chariot from the sky,
Leaves all the world to sleep's refreshing force,
And bids the toiling sons of men to lie.
So too my spirit, vexed by daily strife,
Doth yearn for rest, when shadows longer grow;
To find sweet solace from the cares of life,
And let the troubled thoughts forgotten go.
Then comes the night, with soft and silent tread,
And spreads her velvet mantle o'er the ground;
While in my dreams, by purest fancy led,
My truest comforts, sleeping, I have found.
So let me yield to slumber's kind embrace,
And find in dreams thy sweet and gentle face.


As in a glass, I see the passing years,
The fleeting moments, swift on silent wing;
And mark the havoc, mingled with my tears,
That Time's relentless scythe doth ever bring.
The youthful brow, once smooth and free from care,
Is etched with lines, where sorrows take their hold;
The raven locks, now bleached to silver hair,
A tale of autumn, sadly to be told.
What then avails our beauty, or our might?
When envious age doth steal what youth hath lent?
And plunges all to everlasting night,
The fleeting pleasures, and the moments spent.
But in my verse, thy youth shall ever bloom,
Defying Time, and conquering the tomb.


Though others flatter, with a honeyed tongue,
And build their praise on falsehood's brittle base;
And dedicate to thee a poem sung,
Whose empty words hold not a truth's embrace.
My simple lines, though lacking artful skill,
Are forged in fire, from my heart's own flame;
And echo forth my firm, unwavering will,
To celebrate thy virtue, and thy name.
For truth needs not the paint of gilded lie,
To show its worth, or make its beauty known;
It shines more bright beneath an honest sky,
Than any jewel falsely overgrown.
So take my love, unvarnished and sincere,
A faithful offering, held forever dear.


How heavy do I journey on the way,
When what I seek, my heart hath left behind?
A tiresome trip, to pass each lagging day,
For with my thoughts thy image doth I find.
My eyes do draw thy semblance from my mind,
Which makes them wan and dull with pining gaze,
But when I shut them, then thy form I bind,
And through the darkness thy bright beauty plays.
For then my soul's imaginary sight
Presents thy shape, which takes my breath away;
But ah! this bliss is banished by the light,
And I awake to an unhappy day.
So sleep and dreams my dearest comfort prove,
To bring thee near, whom waking I remove.


The expense of spirit in a waste of shame
Is lust in action; and till action, lust
Is perjured, murderous, bloody, full of blame,
Savage, extreme, rude, cruel, not to trust;
Enjoy'd no sooner but despised straight,
Past reason hunted; and no sooner had,
Past reason hated, as a swallow'd bait,
On purpose laid to make the taker mad.
Mad in pursuit, and in possession so;
Had, having, and in quest to have, extreme;
A bliss in proof, and proved, a very woe;
Before, a joy proposed; behind, a dream.
All this the world well knows; yet none knows well
To shun the heaven that leads men to this hell.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


When I survey the heavens' eternal frame,
And mark the course of each celestial light,
How nature's wisdom doth herself proclaim,
From whose fair influence day derives her right;
When I consider how the wondrous earth
In constant order moves in her estate,
And how the stars, by their harmonious birth,
All do their fixèd destinies await;
Then do I gaze upon thy face, my love,
Which holds a grace beyond all starry spheres,
And swear that thou art sent from powers above,
To still my doubts and banish all my fears.
For in thy beauty dwells a truth so rare,
That heaven's own wonders can with thee compare.


But wherefore do not you a mightier way
Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?
And fortify yourself in your decay
With means more blessed than my barren rhyme?
Now stand you on the top of happy hours,
And many maiden gardens, yet unset,
With virtuous wish would bear your living flowers,
Ere that your date, your very life, be met.
But were it seen, that in this time of woes,
Your image should in canvas fairly stand,
A painted emblem, that the world well knows,
And hold your likeness in a stranger's hand.
This were to conquer time, to be your friend,
And make you live, when you yourself must end.


Thou art so fair, so sweet, and so divine,
That in thy face all heaven's glories meet;
No star in all the firmament doth shine
With such a grace, or essence half so sweet.
Thy lips, like rubies, promise untold joys,
Thy breath, a fragrance from the morning dew;
Thy voice, a charm that all my heart employs,
Each look of thine doth make my life anew.
But oh, thine eyes, like two bright suns at noon,
Eclipse all other lights with their bright ray;
They steal my soul, and leave me swooning soon,
And hold my spirit captive to their sway.
So cruel sweet, so powerfully they bind,
My very self within thy gaze I find.


Let not my love be deem'd a fickle flame,
That doth depend on Fortune's shifting tide,
Nor let my truth be measured by a name,
Which shallow wits do carelessly deride.

For though the seasons change, and flowers fade,
And fleeting clouds obscure the sun's bright ray,
My constant heart, no falsehood hath betrayed,
But holds thy image firm, by night and day.

No outward show, nor Time's remorseless hand,
Shall alter this affection I possess,
Deeper than oceans, firmer than the land,
It knows no bounds, nor can it e'er grow less.

So rests my faith, immutable and deep,
While in my soul thy hallow'd form I keep.


When first thy glorious eyes did meet mine own,
A sudden light did pierce my very soul,
As if a star from heaven had been thrown,
To make my dark and troubled spirit whole.

So potent is the magic in thy gaze,
That reason flees, and passion takes its seat,
And all my former, dull and weary days,
Are banish'd quite, made instantly complete.

No sun, no moon, no fire of mortal art,
Can cast a lustre that with thine may vie,
Thou art the compass of my throbbing heart,
The living heaven reflected in thine eye.

Blest be that glance, which did my life renew,
For in thy sight, I find my truest view.


When I perceive the year in constant round,
From tender bud to winter's stark decay,
How summer's glory fades from verdant ground,
And youthful vigour yields to age's sway.

The blossoming rose, in beauty's briefest reign,
Is quickly pluck'd, or falls by envious frost,
A mirror true of man's ephemeral pain,
Whose precious moments are so quickly lost.

Yet nature's heart doth evermore renew,
Though outward forms may perish and decline,
So should our spirits hold a constant hue,
And not lament, but seek a grace divine.

For even as the seed gives future bloom,
So hope outlives the shadow of the tomb.


Alas, what poor deserts do I possess,
To claim a portion in thy noble thought?
My merit small, my worthiness much less,
Scarce fit to be by gentle fancy sought.

For every grace that in thy person shines,
Doth make my own defects appear more vast,
And in thy beauty, where no blemish pines,
My frailties are to all the world recast.

So doth my spirit, laden with its shame,
Shrink from the light thy peerless virtues shed,
And dares not whisper, even thy dear name,
Lest my rude breath should sully thee instead.

Yet grant me pity, though I ask for naught,
And be my love, by silent suffering caught.


My reason strives to hold its rightful sway,
And bids my heart from foolish thoughts refrain,
To cast thy image from my mind away,
And seek a peace it cannot now attain.

But passion, like a tyrant, holds its ground,
And laughs to scorn what sober sense may preach,
No firm decree, no argument is found,
To curb the longing that my soul doth reach.

For when I think to quell this wild desire,
And call forth wisdom to regain control,
It burns anew, with an intenser fire,
And takes complete possession of my soul.

Thus am I torn, by love's relentless war,
And yield to that which I should most abhor.


When I behold the furrows on my brow,
And mark the traces Time hath left behind,
How swiftly doth my youthful vigour bow,
To shadows born of an aging, weary mind.

My once bright locks, now silver'd by the years,
My strength diminish'd, and my eyesight dim,
Each mirror shows a tale of rising fears,
A fading image, standing at life's brim.

And then I think upon thy beauty's bloom,
Which yet doth hold its unimpaired grace,
And wish that Time, who speeds me to my doom,
Would grant to thee a more enduring space.

But 'tis in vain; his scythe doth spare no man,
And all must yield unto his cruel plan.


When heavy thoughts oppress my troubled breast,
And dark despair doth vex my weary soul,
I find in thee my solace and my rest,
Thou art the haven where my sorrows roll.

For then thy image rises in my sight,
A gentle balm, to soothe my inner pain,
And banishes the shadows of the night,
Making my spirit bright with joy again.

The world outside, with all its bitter strife,
And fortune's blows, which often make me sigh,
Seem but as trifles, in this precious life,
When thy sweet presence doth my heart draw nigh.

So bless'd am I, by this dear love of thine,
Which makes my darkest hours brightly shine.


The lingering night, with all its solemn gloom,
At last gives way to morning's gentle ray,
And from the darkness of a silent room,
I greet the promise of another day.

The mist doth rise from meadows wet with dew,
And sleepy birds begin their joyful tune,
A world reborn, refreshed and fair and new,
Beneath the pale light of the fading moon.

So may my spirit, from its sorrow's hold,
Awaken swift, and cast its grief aside,
Let every tear, a story to be told,
Be wash'd away, by hope's returning tide.

For with the dawn, new possibilities gleam,
And life renews itself, a vibrant dream.


So long as breath shall animate my clay,
Or sight remain to view the sun's bright gleam,
So long as thoughts within my bosom play,
Or memory can hold a cherished dream.

So long as springtime brings the tender bud,
And winter's chill gives way to warmer air,
So long as ocean rolls its mighty flood,
And earth endures, with all its beauty fair.

So long, dear love, shall my affection last,
A flame unquenched, defying Time's decay,
No fleeting fancy, but a love held fast,
Until the judgment of the final day.

And when at last my mortal course is run,
My love for thee shall only be begun.


When envious Time with cruel hand doth seize
The fleeting grace that doth thy youth display,
And steal away thy beauty, and thine ease,
Leaving but wrinkles where thy smiles once lay;
When golden locks turn white with age's frost,
And vibrant spirits yield to failing breath,
And all the bloom of spring seems surely lost,
Consumed by winter's cold and silent death;
Then shall my verse, which seeks thy praise to keep,
Defy the maw of Time's consuming greed,
And though the world in endless slumber sleep,
Thy memory shall plant a living seed.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


The eye of heaven, when it doth most delight,
Doth oft perceive the world through clouded glass,
And fancy's hues obscure what's truly bright,
As fleeting shadows o'er green meadows pass.
So doth my heart, when it beholds thy face,
Doubt if its love be true, or but a dream,
Lost in the labyrinth of time and space,
A ship adrift upon a restless stream.
Yet when thy voice doth charm mine ear with sound,
And gentle touch doth banish every fear,
Within my soul a steady truth is found,
That thou art ever precious, ever dear.
Then let the world revolve in doubt and strife,
My love for thee is anchor to my life.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damasked, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


Accusing heaven, I curse my bitter fate,
That I was born with a too eager mind,
To love so deeply, and to soon abate
The gentle pleasures that my soul doth find.
For often do I grasp at fleeting joys,
And hold them close, as if they were my own,
But like a child who breaks his fragile toys,
I find their brief enchantment quickly flown.
Then in the ruins of my own desire,
I seek a solace that I cannot name,
A hidden spark within a dying fire,
To fan again the passion's fading flame.
But all in vain, for sorrows ever creep,
And leave me nothing but to watch and weep.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


When I behold the fleeting grace of day,
And how the cruel hand of Time doth haste,
To steal the bloom from youth, and beauty's sway,
My thoughts on thy dear loveliness are placed.
For like the summer's flower, thou art so fair,
Yet subject to the winter's frosty breath,
Which doth ere long consume all things that bear,
And gives them over to eternal death.
But in these lines, my love shall never fade,
Nor shall oblivion snatch thy gentle name,
For thou art here in deathless verse arrayed,
And brighter burn'st than any mortal flame.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


My love is as a fever, longing still
For that which longer nurseth the disease;
Feeding upon the hope which doth me kill,
And playing with the pain that gives me ease.
For then I have sworn thee fair, more fair than truth,
And for thy beauty set my reason by;
When my old eyes were dimmed with error's youth,
And knew not what to praise, nor what deny.
But now I see, though too late in the day,
That beauty's veil did hide thy false heart's guile;
And though I loved, I knew not how to say
My true complaint, held captive by thy smile.
Yet love, though blind, sees not the faults it bears,
And so I love thee still, despite my tears.


The weary world, in endless cycle bound,
Doth oft present a glass to man's keen eye,
Where fleeting joys and sorrows can be found,
And Time's swift passage bids all beauty die.
The budding rose, with promise full and bright,
Is but a moment's wonder, then it fades;
And e'en the sun, that lends the day its light,
Must yield its glory to the evening's shades.
So too the grace that decks thy youthful brow,
Shall feel the touch of age, and be consumed;
Unless these verses, penned by fortune's plough,
Preserve thy fairness, from oblivion doomed.
Then let my ink preserve what life doth send,
That thy dear form may know no bitter end.


When lovely youth doth fade and lose its hue,
And time's swift shadows steal away thy light,
Where shall we seek for beauty, fresh and new?
Or guard thee from oblivion's endless night?

The summer's bloom doth swiftly pass away,
And winter's frost doth claim the gentle flower,
So too must every mortal thing decay,
And yield its substance to the hungry hour.

Yet in these lines thy grace shall ever dwell,
A painted image for all eyes to see,
More lasting than a life, a magic spell,
From death's cold grip to set thy spirit free.

So long as men can breathe, or eyes can scan,
My verse shall praise thee, 'gainst time's cruel plan.


Though fickle winds may shift from hour to hour,
And roses change their crimson for a pale,
Thy steadfast mind doth hold a constant power,
That doth not bend though bitter storms assail.

The summer's lease is but a season's span,
And beauty's flower is transient to behold,
So soon it fades, by nature's cruelest plan,
Leaving but winter's shadow, stark and cold.

But thy dear love, a lamp that ever gleams,
Doth light my way through sorrow and through dread,
No fading vision, born of fleeting dreams,
But firm and true, though all the world be dead.

Thus, though all else may fail and fall apart,
Thy truth remains enshrined within my heart.


Why dost thou hoard thy beauty's tender bloom,
And keep it prisoner, meant for all to see?
To what dark end dost thou reserve thy room,
When fading hence, thy grace must swiftly flee?

Like a fair lamp that burns its oil within,
And gives no light to cheer the darkened way,
So thou dost hide the grace thou mightst have been,
And let thy youthful prime thus waste away.

The fleeting hours, like thieves, do steal apace,
And from thy cheek shall pluck the rosy hue,
Then wrinkled age shall mar thy lovely face,
And what was beauty, time shall then undo.

Beget then beauty, ere thou reach thy tomb,
Lest all thy fairness vanish in the gloom.


When Time, the fell destroyer, with his hand,
Shall sweep away all that we deem so fair,
And beauty's structure, built on shifting sand,
Doth vanish quite, dissolving into air;

What then remains of earth's most glorious show?
The proudest towers brought low by slow decay,
And all that blossomed, now lies cold below,
Consumed by shadows of a former day.

No earthly power can stay his swift decree,
Nor gilded monuments his wrath defy,
For he doth claim all things, both wild and free,
And watches kingdoms rise, and then to die.

Yet steadfast love, though all around may cease,
Shall find in faith its never-ending peace.


Those eyes, which hold the heaven's purest light,
And with their beams my darkest soul do grace,
Are stars that guide me through the deepest night,
And banish shadows from this earthly space.

No earthly gem with such a brilliance gleams,
Nor morning dew can hold so fair a hue,
They are the fount of my most cherished dreams,
And in their depths I find my spirit true.

A silent language in their glances plays,
Where truth and virtue exquisitely entwine,
To lead my spirit through life's intricate ways,
And make my mortal essence feel divine.

Oh, blessed sight, that doth my soul enthral,
Thou art my sun, my universe, my all.


The fragile flower doth blossom in the morn,
And by the eve its tender petals fall,
So brief a life, so soon to be forlorn,
Obedient to nature's urgent call.

We gaze upon the sun's declining ray,
And know that darkness soon will fill the air,
Each vibrant moment slips too swift away,
Leaving behind but sorrow and despair.

What then are we, whose days are but a breath?
A transient spark 'twixt darkness and the light,
Each fleeting step doth lead us on to death,
And plunge our being into endless night.

Yet in the heart, love's flame may brightly burn,
And from its ashes, hope shall yet return.


Those eyes, that hold a heaven within their blue,
And with a glance can soothe the troubled breast,
Reflect a soul eternally so true,
And bid my weary spirit find its rest.

No painter's brush, how cunning e'er it be,
Can capture half the magic that they keep,
For in their depths a universe I see,
Where silent thoughts in mystic slumber sleep.

They speak a language that no tongue can frame,
A gentle force that conquers every fear,
And at their light, my troubled heart takes flame,
Dispelling all the shadows dwelling near.

So let them gaze, and let my spirit yield,
To truth and beauty, openly revealed.


Thou art too fair to let thy beauty waste,
A rosebud opening to the morning dew,
Designed by nature, in such lovely haste,
To bless the world with charms forever new.

But if thou dost withhold thy vital spark,
And let thy springtime perish with thy breath,
Then all thy promise fades into the dark,
And gives thy fairness to the maw of death.

For what is beauty, if it bear no fruit?
A treasure buried, never brought to light,
A silent song, plucked from a broken lute,
That fades unheard into the endless night.

Oh, be not cruel, but let thy love increase,
And give to life thy beauty's sweet release.


The transient joy that brightens up my day,
Like morning mist, doth vanish with the sun,
And leaves my spirit desolate to stray,
Ere half its brief, sweet journey is begun.

I seize the moment, holding it so fast,
Lest it escape upon the winged breeze,
But even as I grasp, it is past,
And I am left with sorrow and unease.

The laughter loud, the jest, the merry tune,
How quickly do they fade into the air,
Beneath the gaze of an indifferent moon,
And leave behind but echoes of despair.

So learn, my soul, that bliss is but a guest,
And in its absence, patience is the test.


Behold the ocean, restless and profound,
Whose mighty waves defy the human will,
No fragile bark upon its bosom bound,
Can long withstand its power, surging still.

The mountains stand, colossal, proud, and grand,
Unchanged through ages, heedless of man's plight,
While kingdoms rise and fall throughout the land,
They pierce the heavens with eternal light.

The storm-tossed cloud, that doth the tempest bring,
Unleashes fury on the trembling earth,
No mortal art can clip its mighty wing,
Nor alter nature's elemental birth.

So small are we, beneath creation's span,
A fleeting shadow in God's cosmic plan.


When heavy thoughts do press upon my soul,
And sorrow's clouds obscure life's joyous view,
My weary spirit yields to their control,
And finds no comfort, save in thoughts of you.

For in thy friendship, I a haven find,
A shelter safe from every bitter storm,
A gentle solace for my troubled mind,
That keeps my doubting heart forever warm.

No flattering tongue, nor false and hollow praise,
Can stir such loyalty as thine doth show,
Thou light'st my path through life's perplexing maze,
And help'st my burdened spirit onward go.

So blessèd be the hour when first we met,
A faithful friend, I never shall forget.


When fleeting beauty fades to dust away,
And youth's bright spark doth vanish from the gaze,
What hand can stay the ruin of decay,
Or rescue grace from time's consuming maze?

The sculptor's art may carve a noble form,
And painter's skill immortal hues bestow,
But these are frail, defenseless 'gainst the storm,
And yield to winter's unforgiving snow.

Yet in these humble lines, thy spirit bright
Shall find a mirror, radiant and clear,
A beacon shining through eternal night,
Dispelling every doubt and every fear.

For in this verse, thy truth shall ever last,
Long after earthly glories are past.


How can I swear my love shall always hold,
When nature's law decrees that all must fade?
The freshest flower turns to withered mold,
And beauty's promise is too soon betrayed.

No oath can bind the fickle turning tide,
Nor fervent vow arrest the winged hour,
For time doth laugh at pledges, side by side,
And plucks the petals from the strongest flower.

Yet in this doubt, my faith doth still endure,
That what is true, no moment can erase,
A constant flame, eternally so pure,
That finds its solace in thy gentle grace.

Though all things change, and swiftly pass away,
My love for thee shall know no changing day.


As perfect form as ever nature made,
Endowed with beauty, truth, and gentle mien,
No flaw within thy lovely mold is laid,
Thou art the fairest that was ever seen.

The morning sun doth borrow from thine eye
A brighter lustre for his golden ray,
And stars at night, when thou art passing by,
Do pale their fires, ashamed to light the way.

No artifice doth paint thy rosy cheek,
Nor borrowed tints thy peerless grace impart,
But all thy charms, a silent language speak,
And captivate each longing, loving heart.

So wholly good, so utterly divine,
All excellence in thee doth brightly shine.


The breath of dawn, which doth the darkness chase,
Doth oft precede a day of cloudy gloom;
So joy doth vanish, leaving sorrow's trace,
And hopeful flowers give way to dusty tomb.
What trust may frail humanity repose
In earthly gifts, which swift as shadows fly?
A transient bliss, which no man truly knows,
Ere it doth fade beneath a weeping sky.
Yet in thy truth, a steadfast star I find,
A guiding light when all the world seems lost,
A balm to soothe the troubled, weary mind,
Worth more than empire, or a kingdom's cost.
For in thy heart, no fleeting change I see,
But constancy, which bindeth me to thee.


When nature's cunning hand with lavish store
Hath wrought the forms of beauty to behold,
She doth but lend, and asks for payment more,
And all her treasures turn to dust and mould.
The fairest flower, that lifts its gentle head,
Must soon resign its glory to the ground;
And all the vibrant life, so richly fed,
By winter's icy breath is quickly drowned.
But thou, whose essence shines beyond decay,
Whose spirit knows no fading or decline,
Dost banish shadows with thy glorious ray,
A lasting monument, a soul divine.
So, though the world its fleeting shows doth boast,
My constant heart to thee is given most.


How oft I sigh, when absence holds thee far,
And cruel distance doth our souls divide!
Each sun-lit day seems like a dreary star,
And shadows lengthen where thy joys abide.
My weary thoughts, like pilgrims, take their flight,
To seek thy presence in some distant land;
They find thee not, and sink in darkest night,
And leave my spirit chained by sorrow's hand.
Then do I chide the sun for shining bright,
And curse the moon that lights a lonely way,
Wishing for swift return of morning's light,
To bring thee back, and banish my dismay.
Come back, my love, and banish every fear,
For joy returns when thy sweet form is near.


When envious tongues thy spotless name assail,
And malice seeks thy reputation's stain,
My loyal heart shall never turn nor quail,
But rise to prove thy virtue once again.
They know thee not, who judge by outward show,
Nor pierce the depths where truth and honour lie;
Their shallow minds no deeper currents know,
But by false rumour let their judgments fly.
But I, who know the essence of thy soul,
Whose every thought doth mirror truth's pure light,
Will stand for thee, though storms around us roll,
And champion thy cause with all my might.
For slander's breath, howe'er it strives to sting,
Cannot corrupt the truth that thou dost bring.


My mind, like to a restless, feathered bird,
Doth seek its solace in thy gentle gaze;
At every absence, its deep cry is heard,
And pines for comforts of thy sunlit days.
No painted scene, nor beauty wrought by art,
Can hold its gaze as can thy living grace;
No dulcet tone can soothe my troubled heart,
Like but a whisper from thy lovely face.
For in thy form, all heaven's glories dwell,
A mirror where my truest joys I see;
A magic charm, a sweet and potent spell,
That binds my yearning spirit fast to thee.
So let me dwell where thy dear presence lies,
And lose myself within thy loving eyes.


The merchant, fearing rocks and ocean's rage,
Secures his goods against the watery tomb;
The miser hoards his gold from every age,
Lest poverty should seal his bitter doom.
But I, whose treasure doth in thee reside,
Can find no fortress strong enough to keep
Thy precious worth safe from the world's harsh tide,
Nor wakeful guard against oblivion's sleep.
Save this alone: that in these lines of mine,
Thy peerless beauty shall forever bloom;
A living spirit, deathless and divine,
Escaping time's inexorable gloom.
So let the world its transient riches crave,
My greater wealth in thee, this verse shall save.


When I behold the proudest works of man,
The crumbling tower, the palace turned to dust,
I see how frail is every earthly plan,
And how all glory yields to time's slow rust.
The mightiest oak, that braves a hundred years,
Must finally bow beneath the tempest's hand;
The crystal stream, that through the valley steers,
Is lost within the vastness of the sand.
And then I think upon thy fleeting grace,
Thy youthful beauty, destined to decay;
And how death's shadow soon will hide thy face,
And sweep thy loveliness from earth away.
Unless these lines a monument can be,
To grant eternal life, my love, to thee.


My heart, once swift and eager for delight,
Now beats a measured rhythm, calm and low;
Its youthful fires, quenched by time's cold night,
No longer with such ardent passion glow.
The dreams that once like soaring eagles flew,
Now perch upon the bough of sober thought;
And all the false allurements I once knew,
By wiser judgment are to nothing brought.
Yet in this change, a deeper truth I find,
A clearer vision of what truly lasts;
And unto thee, my constant, faithful mind,
Its anchor holds against life's bitter blasts.
For love like ours, refined by passing years,
Outlives the tide of earthly hopes and fears.


The gaudy pomp that earthly power doth wield,
And brittle fame that fades with swift decline,
Cannot compare to truth by thee revealed,
A lasting glory, purer and divine.
Let kings command, and empires rise and fall,
Let common tongues speak praises to the great;
Their fleeting triumphs answer nature's call,
And bow to destiny's unyielding state.
But thy integrity, a constant flame,
Burns ever bright, untouched by worldly strife;
And virtue's crown doth consecrate thy name,
A monument to everlasting life.
So let me cling to what is truly strong,
And in thy truth, my spirit shall belong.


My thoughts, like clouds that drift across the sky,
Are ever changing, with each passing breeze;
They seek new wonders, wheresoe'er they fly,
And find small comfort in life's transient ease.
But when they turn, dear love, to think on thee,
A sudden stillness settles on my breast;
And all the restless spirit finds decree,
In thy calm haven, where it finds its rest.
No anxious doubt then clouds my inner sight,
No vain desire can tempt my heart astray;
For in thy goodness, shining ever bright,
I find a solace for each passing day.
So let my mind on thee forever dwell,
And break the binding of each earthly spell.


When gentle sleep hath sealed my weary eyes,
And dreams, like shadows, flit across my mind,
Thy image then, in softened guise, doth rise,
And leaves all waking sorrows far behind.
I walk with thee through groves of endless green,
Where silver streams in murmuring music flow;
And in thy smile, a joy I've rarely seen,
Doth set my slumbering spirit all aglow.
But when the cock's shrill crow doth break my rest,
And morning's light dispels the dream's sweet haze,
A sudden sorrow fills my aching breast,
To find thee gone, in life's accustomed ways.
Ah, would that sleep could hold thee ever near,
And banish waking's cold and bitter tear!


O, what a grief, to see the world grow cold,
And summer's vibrant beauty turn to grey!
The tales of youth by wrinkled age are told,
As swiftly all things hasten to decay.
The golden locks, that once did softly gleam,
Are touched with silver, by time's chilling hand;
And bright eyes dim, as from a waking dream,
As memory fades, like prints upon the sand.
Yet in these lines, a solace I have found,
A sacred space where beauty may reside;
Where thy fair image, ever freshly crowned,
Shall still endure, though centuries have died.
So live thou here, in everlasting prime,
Defying death, and conquering all time.


When cruel Time, with wrinkles, mars the brow,
And steals away the beauty of thy youth,
Then shall I ask if still thou lov'st me now,
And find within thy faithful heart the truth.
For transient joys, like blossoms, quickly die,
And earthly grace doth fade as swiftly fast,
But love, which dwells within the constant eye,
A firmer bond than fleeting days can cast.
So let the seasons bring their chilling change,
And wintry blasts upon the branches blow,
My steadfast heart shall know no wider range,
But ever in thy constant favour grow.
    Thus, whilst I live, thy worth shall I proclaim,
    And deathless verse shall guard thy precious name.


The mind, distraught with cares of earthly things,
Doth oft forget the solace of true love;
And fleeting joys, on borrowed, airy wings,
Do bear us far from blessings from above.
But when thy gentle visage doth appear,
A sudden calm doth on my spirit fall,
Dispelling doubt and vanquishing all fear,
And making sense of life's discordant call.
For in thine eyes, a steadfast light I find,
That guides my steps through shadows of the night,
A beacon true, a haven for my mind,
And fills my soul with ever-fresh delight.
    Let worldly storms rage on with fierce disdain,
    My love for thee shall constantly remain.


When I behold the heavens' starry sphere,
And mark the wanderings of the moon so pale,
I then recall thy beauty, held so dear,
Whose gentle light doth over all prevail.
For greater lustre in thy face doth dwell,
Than all the gems that crown the brow of night,
And with a charm, no mortal tongue can tell,
Thou dost illuminate my spirit's sight.
No greater joy mine eye hath e'er surveyed,
No sweeter solace found my troubled breast,
Than by thy grace, my weary soul is swayed,
And in thy presence finds its perfect rest.
    Thus, heaven's wonders though they brightly gleam,
    Are but a shadow of thy beauteous dream.


The gentle spring, with blossoms freshly born,
Doth deck the fields in robes of verdant hue;
And nature wakes to greet the early morn,
With fragrant breath, and skies of clearest blue.
Yet all this beauty, though it charms the eye,
Holds not the power thy gentle grace doth hold;
For in thy face, more heavenly tints do lie,
Than all the treasures earth can e'er unfold.
The rose may blush, the lily show its white,
The violet may its sweet perfume impart,
But none can match thy pure, celestial light,
That shines with truth within thy tender heart.
    So, when I gaze on thee, all else grows dim,
    And praise for thee becomes my only hymn.


My love for thee, a fervent, constant fire,
Doth burn within my soul with endless gleam;
It doth transcend all earthly, base desire,
And makes my waking life a blessed dream.
For in thy virtues, I behold a light,
That shames the sun, and puts the stars to flight;
Thou art my guide through shadows of the night,
My refuge true, my solace and delight.
No hardship e'er can make my spirit quail,
No bitter tempest shake my firm resolve,
My steadfast heart shall never, never fail,
As circling seasons endlessly revolve.
    So long as breath doth animate my frame,
    So long shall I adore thy sacred name.


The proudest towers, that pierce the azure sky,
Do yield at last to time's relentless sway;
And mortal grandeur, made to catch the eye,
Like summer flowers, doth quickly fade away.
But love, unmov'd by fortune's fickle spin,
Doth scorn the triumphs that ambition craves;
Its realm is found, eternally, within,
Beyond the reach of all life's stormy waves.
Let earthly kings contend for fleeting power,
And chase the shadows of an empty fame;
I ask no more than in thy loving bower,
To cherish still thy sweet and gentle name.
    For in thy heart, my truest riches lie,
    A treasure brighter than the stars on high.


When I consider life's uncertain stage,
And how each moment swiftly takes its flight,
From youth's bright morn to sorrow's weary age,
And all is swallowed in eternal night;
I mark the rose, how fair it blooms today,
Yet ere the sun hath run its daily course,
Its vibrant petals swiftly fall away,
Succumbing to decay's relentless force.
So, too, doth beauty fade, and strength decline,
And all that's cherished yields to dusty doom;
No mortal power can stay this swift design,
But all must journey to the silent tomb.
    Yet in my heart, thy image shall remain,
    Proof 'gainst the ravage of time's bitter pain.


My spirit, oft in melancholy bound,
Doth wander lost through labyrinths of doubt;
No sweet repose my troubled soul hath found,
Until thy radiant presence puts them out.
Like barren lands, that thirst for gentle rain,
My parched desires long only for thy sight;
To ease the burden of my secret pain,
And fill my darkened world with glorious light.
For in thy glance, a healing balm I feel,
A magic touch that makes my spirit whole;
Thy tender words, like music, sweetly steal,
And bring a solace to my weary soul.
    Thus, by thy grace, transformed I stand anew,
    And find my truest self, reflected true.


The golden sun, that warms the fertile earth,
Doth call forth blossoms of a fleeting hue;
They come to life, enjoy their brief, bright birth,
Then bid the world a sorrowful adieu.
So too, doth beauty, born of flesh and blood,
A transient glory, destined to decay;
No art can stay the swiftly flowing flood,
That bears all mortal loveliness away.
But hadst thou borne a child, a living grace,
Thy fairer form would not be lost to blight;
A second self, to fill thy vacant place,
And keep thy memory shining ever bright.
    Then, though thou fade, thy beauty would survive,
    And in thy offspring, brightly would it live.


No earthly jewel, however rich and rare,
Can match the lustre of thy gentle mind;
No painted canvas, beautiful and fair,
Reflects the virtues that in thee I find.
For wisdom dwells within thy thoughtful breast,
And kindness guides thy words, and all thy ways;
Thy generous spirit puts my heart at rest,
And fills my lonely, solitary days.
The world may praise the fleeting shows of might,
And shallow fame may crown the proud and bold,
But thine's a grace, whose pure and constant light,
Is worth more than a mine of purest gold.
    Thus, in thy truth, a perfect joy I see,
    And owe my best of praises unto thee.


When Death, that hungry tyrant, comes to claim
The fairest forms, and lays all beauty low,
And seeks to blot from memory thy name,
And steal the grace that makes thy spirit glow;
Then shall my love, a shield against his power,
Defy his scythe, and mock his empty boast;
And in these lines, beyond his darkest hour,
Thy deathless virtues shall be treasured most.
For love, more strong than any mortal might,
Can build a fortress 'gainst the gaping tomb;
And cast upon thy worth a lasting light,
To triumph over sorrow and all gloom.
    So shalt thou live, when marble turns to dust,
    Preserv'd in lines of truth and endless trust.


When trials come, and burdens press me sore,
And worldly cares besiege my weary mind,
I turn to thee, and ask for nothing more,
Than in thy gentle presence peace to find.
For in this world, where strife and envy dwell,
And falsehood often wears a pleasing face,
Thy steadfast heart doth cast a potent spell,
And fills my anxious spirit with sweet grace.
Let others chase the fleeting breath of fame,
And seek for riches in a barren field;
I find my joy in speaking of thy name,
And know to whom my loyalties must yield.
    So, come what may, through sunshine or through storm,
    Thy constant love shall keep my spirit warm.


When first I saw thy face, a wondrous sight,
My soul was captivated by its gleam;
As morning breaks, and chases darkest night,
So did thy beauty banish every dream.
For in thine eyes, a gentle fire did burn,
And on thy lips, a sweet and modest grace;
My restless heart no longer yearned to turn,
But found its haven in that lovely place.
No earthly art can capture such a hue,
No poet's words describe thy perfect mien;
Thou art a vision, beautiful and true,
A peerless mistress, and a matchless queen.
    So, when I gaze on thee, my senses reel,
    And all the depth of boundless love I feel.


The shifting seasons, in their constant round,
Bring forth their beauty, and their sad decay;
From winter's slumber, silent, cold, and bound,
To summer's warmth, that soon must pass away.
The budding spring, with promise in its air,
Gives place to summer, crowned with golden light;
Then autumn paints the trees beyond compare,
Before the coming of the icy night.
Yet 'midst this change, where all things yield to time,
My constant love doth steadfastly remain;
Unmoved by seasons, or by frost or rime,
It blooms eternal, 'midst joy and pain.
    For though the world may alter and decline,
    My faithful heart is ever truly thine.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


O from what power hast thou this powerful might,
With insufficiency my heart to sway?
To make me live upon thy frown or light,
And with a word my spirit to dismay?
I know not what, but when I look on thee,
A strange compulsion binds my captive will,
That though my soul desires to wander free,
It finds itself enthrall'd by thy sweet skill.
Thy voice, thy form, thy every gentle grace,
Doth hold me fast in bonds I can't undo,
And though I strive to flee this charming place,
My faithful heart will ever turn to you.
So in this thrall, content I will remain,
And count my service as my greatest gain.


From those bright orbs, where golden light doth dwell,
My soul draws solace in its deepest night,
A silent language that no tongue can tell,
A star to guide me with its gentle might.

For in their depths, a universe resides,
Of truths unspoken, mysteries profound,
Where virtue's pure and gentle spirit hides,
And peace eternal may at once be found.

Oft have I watch'd their sympathetic gleam,
When sorrow's shadow cross'd my troubled face,
Dispelling darkness, like a waking dream,
And filling all my being with sweet grace.

Let envious Time their lustre never steal,
For in their gaze, my very self I feel.


When dull invention seeks in vain to rise,
And sluggish thoughts refuse to take their flight,
My weary pen then doth no words devise,
But wanders lost within the paper's white.

Then do I call upon my dearest Muse,
Whose sacred fire can make the dullest gleam,
And from her fount, new inspiration choose,
To fashion verse as bright as any dream.

She doth impart a vigour to my hand,
And bids my spirit soar on joyful wing,
Unlocks the secrets of each fertile land,
And teaches every heartfelt note to sing.

Without her aid, my labours were in vain,
But with her touch, I live and write again.


Behold the spring, how brief her sweet domain,
When tender buds their emerald robes unfold,
And blossoms bright do grace the verdant plain,
Ere autumn's breath doth turn them pale and cold.

The cuckoo's call, the nightingale's soft lay,
A fleeting melody in summer's air,
Shall swiftly pass, and vanish with the day,
Leaving the boughs of winter bleak and bare.

So too, our joys, like flowers of the field,
Do bloom awhile, then wither and decay,
And all that beauty Nature's bounty yield,
Is but a moment's grace, then stol'n away.

But in remembrance, beauty may reside,
A treasure held, though all the seasons glide.


When reckless youth, with headstrong, fiery zeal,
Doth rush headlong where caution fears to tread,
And wounds itself, unmindful how to heal,
By empty hopes and foolish passions led,

Then doth experience, stern but kindly guide,
Unfold the lessons by past errors taught,
And bids the soul lay youthful scorn aside,
To seek the treasures wisely to be sought.

For Time, the master, though he steal our grace,
Doth also bring a clearer, wiser sight,
And paints new lines upon a thoughtful face,
Reflecting truth, dispelling folly's night.

So let us learn, and cast our faults away,
And greet with knowledge each succeeding day.


When weary flesh its daily toil hath done,
And anxious thoughts do vex the troubled brain,
Then gentle Sleep, by Nature's hand begun,
Doth draw her curtain o'er life's bitter pain.

She binds the senses in a silken thread,
And lays the mind in peaceful slumber deep,
Consigns to rest the living and the dead,
And secrets from the waking world doth keep.

A sweet oblivion, from all cares set free,
A brief release from sorrow's heavy chain,
That bids the soul in placid dreams to flee,
And grants new strength to face the day again.

So let us yield unto her soft command,
And wake refresh'd, by her restoring hand.


The year doth turn, and seasons swiftly glide,
From verdant spring to summer's golden heat,
Then autumn's bounty, where rich fruits abide,
Till winter's chill makes nature's heart retreat.

Each brings its beauty, transient and unique,
The budding leaf, the blossom's fragrant breath,
The harvest store, the snow-clad, frozen peak,
A cycle marking life, and hinting death.

So doth our life, through various stages run,
From childhood's morn to youth's tempestuous noon,
Till mellow age completes what was begun,
Beneath the pale and silent, watching moon.

Let us embrace each change, as fate doth send,
And learn contentment till life's journey end.


The world doth prize the glitter and the show,
