The outward garb, the rich and costly stone,
And rarely seeks the hidden worth below,
Where virtue's quiet essence may be known.

For oft a heart that beats with modest grace,
Enshrines more honour than a king's array,
And purer beauty in a humble face,
Than all the jewels wealth can e'er display.

Let shallow minds pursue the empty praise,
And fawn on greatness, howsoe'er it rise,
But give me truth, in unadorned ways,
That shines with light, reflected from the skies.

For true nobility, in spirit dwells,
Beyond the pomp that worldly grandeur sells.


No summer's day can boast thy grace entire,
Nor gentle breeze can match thy spirit free,
Nor blooming rose possess such sweet desire,
As doth within thy lovely being be.

The fleeting cloud, that drifts across the blue,
Though bright its edges, soon will fade from sight,
And transient gleams, like morning's tender hue,
Are lost when day asserts its fuller light.

So fragile beauty, born of earthly dust,
Doth quickly wane, and know its fading hour,
And all that seems so fair, so sweet, so just,
Succumbs at last to Time's consuming power.

But truth of heart, and virtue's firm control,
Shall ever shine within a constant soul.


When last of breath from mortal coil doth part,
And silent dust doth claim what once was bright,
When cold oblivion stills the vibrant heart,
And draws a curtain on life's fading light,

Then shall the grave, with solemn, dark embrace,
Hold firm thy form, removed from earthly view,
And Time's relentless hand shall quite efface,
The living image, vibrant, fresh, and new.

But fear thou not, though flesh to earth descend,
And spirit take its flight to realms unknown,
For in these lines, thy memory shall transcend,
And through my verse, thy deathless worth be shown.

So shalt thou live, when marble tombs decay,
And conquer death, through this immortal lay.


When summer's lease is done and autumn nigh,
And youthful beauty doth begin to fade,
Then cruel time doth steal from every eye,
The rosy blush that nature once displayed.
For wrinkled age will conquer all too fast,
And steal the glory from thy golden hair,
The strength of youth, too quickly it is past,
Leaving but shadows of what once was fair.
Yet, in these lines, thy grace shall ever dwell,
A monument against time's ravening maw,
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can spell,
Thy beauty shall transcend all mortal law.
Thus, though thy form to dust must turn and cease,
My verse shall grant thy memory sweet release.


Mine eyes do crave the sight they cannot gain,
My heart doth ache for what it holds so dear,
And every moment brings but greater pain,
Since thou, my life, no longer dost appear.
Like one who seeks a star in deepest night,
And finds but clouds that mock his yearning gaze,
So do I wander, lost for want of light,
Through gloomy valleys and through sunless days.
Oh, absence, tyrant, thou dost sting and wound,
And make my spirits sink to lowest ebb,
No solace in this wretched world is found,
Caught in despair's intricate, endless web.
Return, sweet love, and banish sorrow's sting,
Or cease my breath, and end what suffering bring.


Why should my heart so oft be vexed and sore,
When gentle skies do beam with golden light?
And why my spirit troubled to its core,
When nature paints the day with hues so bright?
'Tis not the world, nor fortunes cruel decree,
That plants these thorns within my troubled breast,
But inward thoughts, that ceaseless torture me,
Denying peace and banishing all rest.
Oh, wretched mind, that canst not find repose,
But feeds on shadows, born of doubt and fear,
Each joy it finds, a darker sorrow shows,
And drowns all comfort in a bitter tear.
Then let me sleep, and dream of happier things,
Until a truer dawn new promise brings.


When first I saw thy face, a radiant gleam,
My soul, enthralled, did mark thee for its own,
As poets gaze upon a waking dream,
And find therein a beauty never known.
Thy eyes, like stars, did cast a gentle fire,
That warmed my blood and set my heart ablaze,
Inspiring thoughts that lift my spirit higher,
And chase away the gloom of former days.
No painted mask, no artifice of skill,
Could e'er approach the grace that dwells in thee;
Thou art thyself, beyond a painter's will,
A living wonder for all men to see.
Then let thy light forever guide my way,
And turn my darkest night to brightest day.


Comparing thee to roses, fresh and fair,
Whose tender petals ope to greet the sun,
I find a beauty none can quite compare,
When summer's fleeting pageant is begun.
But roses fade, their brief perfection done,
And scattered leaves confess their swift decay,
When winter's icy conquest hath been won,
Their fragrant glory stolen far away.
Yet thy dear charm, no season can impair,
No blighting frost can dim thy vibrant hue;
My love for thee doth banish all despair,
And keeps thy verdant spirit ever new.
So long as breath in mortal men shall bide,
Thy changeless beauty shall with verse reside.


My love is like the tide, that ebbs and flows,
Now full of ardour, now with cold despair;
A fickle wind that as it listeth, blows,
And leaves my heart to wrestle with its care.
One moment, blest by thy approving smile,
I feel my spirit soar to heights above;
The next, thy frown, with treachery and guile,
Doth cast me down from all my hopeful love.
Oh, constant heart, where can such refuge be?
From shifting sands of change and false delight?
To find a truth, unwavering and free,
To anchor thoughts through day and darkest night.
But since thou art so changeful, so untrue,
My heart must learn to live apart from you.


If nature's gifts, which time doth quickly waste,
Were not renewed, but perished with each hour,
Then beauty's lineage would be quite erased,
And lovely forms would lose their vital power.
But wisely she doth teach us to impart
Our fairest substance to a younger race,
To engrave our essence on a willing heart,
And leave behind a legacy of grace.
Then heed her call, let not thy beauty die,
But make some fair copy, ere thy life be done,
That future ages, when they gaze thereby,
May see thy charms beneath a youthful sun.
So shalt thou live, though dust thy body claim,
Preserved forever in another's name.


The gaudy spring, with blossoms freshly strewn,
Doth yield its sweetness to the summer's hold;
And summer's pomp, beneath the harvest moon,
Gives way to autumn's russet, brown, and gold.
Then winter comes, with icy breath and chill,
And strips the trees of every verdant leaf,
A silent promise of a spring to fill
The barren branches, bringing sweet relief.
So too our lives, in seasons do unfold,
From tender youth to age's hoary brow;
A story written, centuries old,
With fleeting joys, and sorrows we allow.
But love, like spring, shall ever bloom anew,
If faithful hearts remain both firm and true.


My love is just, though others may deride,
My faith unshaken, though the storms may blow;
No envious tongue can turn my heart aside,
No cruel rumour make my feelings low.
For I have known thy spirit, pure and bright,
And seen the truth that shines within thine eyes,
A constant star, through sorrow's darkest night,
A haven safe from all that life denies.
Let fools condemn, and slanderers invent,
Their shallow words can never reach my soul;
My steadfast heart on thee is wholly bent,
And shall remain, though ages onward roll.
For in thy love, my truest self I find,
Leaving the shifting world and doubts behind.


What tongue can speak the wonders of thy grace?
What mortal pen can capture such a light?
My halting words cannot thy worth embrace,
Nor paint thy beauty, shining ever bright.
My humble Muse, though earnest in its plea,
Doth struggle hard to form a fitting praise,
For all its efforts fall far short of thee,
And falter 'midst thy soul's resplendent blaze.
Forgive, dear love, this poor and weak attempt,
To limn a form that Heaven's hand did mould;
From all false flattery I stand exempt,
My simple truth is more than finest gold.
Though words may fail, my heart shall ever try,
To show the love that cannot fade or die.


Let fickle fate her cruelest blows impart,
And scatter joys like leaves before the wind;
Let envious tongues conspire to break my heart,
And leave my spirit utterly maligned.
Yet shall my love for thee remain as firm
As rooted oak that scorns the winter's rage,
Unchanged by time, unyielding to the worm,
A deathless truth on life's bewildering page.
For thou art more than transient beauty's show,
More than the fleeting pleasures of an hour;
Within thy soul, such constant virtues grow,
That time itself shall not possess the power.
To dim my faith, or quench this sacred flame,
Which burns eternal, whispering thy name.


My love for thee doth mock the passing hour,
And scoffs at time, which all things else consumes,
It stands untouched by winter's icy power,
And laughs at summer's heat and autumn's fumes.
For what is beauty but a fleeting dream,
A momentary flash upon the stage,
If not upheld by love's eternal gleam,
Which binds the heart through every changing age?
Let fading roses shed their petals frail,
Let ancient stones succumb to slow decay,
My constant heart shall never know such fail,
But burn more brightly with each passing day.
So long as breath in mortal body dwells,
My truth for thee a timeless story tells.


When fickle Fortune turns her wheel unkind,
And lays her heavy hand on mortal state,
Or grief and care do vex the troubled mind,
And joy's bright promise meets an early fate;
Then do I turn to lines of sacred art,
Where poets' souls their deathless thoughts unfold,
And find a solace for my weary heart,
A treasury of wisdom, rich and old.
For though the body crumbles into dust,
And fading breath doth leave its earthly cell,
The spirit's light, preserved by pen and trust,
Through ages dark, its wondrous tale shall tell.
So let my verse, though humble, play its part,
To keep thy memory deep within the heart.


Upon thy brow, no wrinkle yet appears,
Nor time's cruel hand hath stolen any grace,
Thy youthful eye doth banish all my fears,
And in thy smile, I see a tranquil place.
Thy cheek, a rose where blushing beauty dwells,
Thy voice, a music sweeter than the spring,
More captivating than a siren's spells,
The solace that my troubled heart doth bring.
Let cynics scoff at beauty's swift decay,
And preachers rail 'gainst earthly, vain delight,
I find in thee a perfect, sunlit day,
That casts a radiance on the darkest night.
Such heavenly form, a wonder to behold,
A treasure richer far than gems or gold.


Why do mine eyes, once bright with cheerful hue,
Now shed such bitter tears without control?
What unseen sorrow doth my peace subdue,
And cast a shadow on my inmost soul?
Perhaps the past, with spectres grim and deep,
Returns to haunt the quiet of my rest,
Or future fears, their silent vigil keep,
And press with leaden weight upon my breast.
Oh, would that I could banish every grief,
And bid despair to leave my weary sight,
To find in slumber some profound relief,
And wake refreshed to dawn's returning light.
But till that day, my spirit must contend,
With woes whose bitter pangs do never end.


When thou art far, a dreary silence reigns,
And joy itself seems banished from my door,
My heart doth echo with a lover's pains,
And yearns to see thy cherished face once more.
The sun shines bright, the birds sing sweet and clear,
But all these pleasures seem a hollow show,
For absence chills the spirit with its fear,
And doth a constant, silent sorrow sow.
I walk alone, where once we used to stray,
And every spot recalls thy gentle tread,
A thousand memories light the lonely way,
And whisper softly of the words we said.
Return, my love, and banish all my strife,
For thou alone canst bring my heart to life.


Behold the lily, in its purest white,
That doth with grace adorn the verdant field,
Or evening stars, that gem the velvet night,
To whom all other earthly glories yield.
The lark, with song, doth greet the rising sun,
The gentle stream doth murmur on its way,
Each wondrous marvel, 'neath the heaven's run,
Doth praise its Maker with each passing day.
Yet none of these, though beautiful and grand,
Can match the grace that in thy form I find,
A perfect master-piece from nature's hand,
Designed to charm the eye and bless the mind.
In thee, all earthly beauties seem to meet,
A vision perfect, wondrous, and complete.


The fairest face may hide a false design,
And polished words conceal a wicked heart,
A poisoned chalice often gleams divine,
And with sweet show doth play a treacherous part.
For outward grace doth often lead astray,
And tempt the eye with beauty's hollow guise,
While truth itself may choose a plainer way,
And shun the flattery that beguiles the wise.
Let not thy judgment be by show misled,
Nor trust the surface of a painted scene,
For often virtue's path is humbly spread,
And honesty doth wear a sober mien.
Seek not the glitter, which doth soon decay,
But steadfast worth, that lasts beyond a day.


When I behold the swift decay of things,
And how the strongest walls must turn to dust,
The proudest towers that aspiration brings,
Beneath time's slow and conquering rust;
When verdant spring gives way to summer's heat,
And summer's glory fades to autumn's chill,
And wintry blasts the year's last hopes defeat,
I ponder on the fate that waits us still.
The fleeting breath, the body's fragile form,
The certain end that every mortal knows,
How transient life, enduring but a storm,
Before to quiet earth its spirit goes.
This thought alone doth chill my very bone,
That all we cherish, soon will be outgrown.


When sorrows strike, and fortune proves unkind,
And heavy burdens press upon my soul,
To thee, my friend, my weary steps I find,
And in thy presence, feel my spirit whole.
For thou dost share my griefs with gentle grace,
And lend an ear to every whispered fear,
A comfort shining from thy honest face,
That doth dispel each dark and bitter tear.
No flatterer art thou, with specious art,
But true as steel, a steadfast, loyal guide,
Who holds a cherished place within my heart,
Where trust and truth forevermore abide.
Such friendship rare, a gift from heaven above,
A bond unbreakable, a constant love.


My restless thoughts, like birds with weary wing,
Do seek thy presence through the fading light,
And find no solace that the world can bring,
When thou art absent from my longing sight.
Each moment passed, an age of bitter pain,
Each silent hour, a burden to endure,
My soul doth thirst, like fields for gentle rain,
And yearns for joys that only thou canst cure.
The very air seems heavy with despair,
The colours dim, the music cold and strange,
For all delight is fled beyond compare,
When cruel distance doth our lives estrange.
Come quickly back, and end this mournful plea,
Restore my heart, and set my spirit free.


Let others boast of beauty, riches, might,
And seek their praise in transient, earthly show,
I value truth, which shines with purest light,
And doth a lasting, inner peace bestow.
For outward glitter often doth deceive,
And painted masks conceal a shallow mind,
While honest hearts have no need to conceive
A subtle web of falsehoods intertwined.
The noble spirit, clad in simple grace,
Doth greater worth than any king command,
And leaves a legacy no time can chase,
A beacon shining brightly through the land.
So let my life reflect this sacred rule,
To be sincerely true, and no man's fool.


My gentle muse, from whence thy power flows,
To stir my heart and bid my spirit sing?
A subtle magic that my being knows,
A silent voice that doth its comfort bring.
When heavy clouds obscure the mind's clear sight,
And inspiration hides its sacred flame,
Thou dost return, a vision pure and bright,
And softly whisper forth thy cherished name.
The words unbidden rise, in rhythmic flow,
The thoughts take wing, like doves into the air,
A tapestry of beauty starts to grow,
Dispelling shadows, banishing despair.
So bless me still, with thy celestial art,
And let thy music dwell within my heart.


Though cruel time may plough furrows in thy face,
And youth's bright summer yield to winter's cold,
Though fleeting beauty find no lasting place,
And all that's cherished slowly turns to old;
My steadfast love shall mock at such decay,
And hold thee precious, though thy bloom be past,
For in thy spirit, dwells a brighter day,
A noble essence, meant for aye to last.
Let worldly eyes perceive a fading grace,
And turn their gaze to novel, fresher forms,
My loyal heart shall find in thy embrace,
A refuge safe from life's tempestuous storms.
For what I love, is not a transient show,
But depth of soul, where endless virtues grow.


When I do muse on beauty's fleeting show,
And see the spring adorn with blossoms gay,
How summer's pride doth to the autumn go,
And all fair things must fade and pass away.
Then think I on thy grace, which now doth bloom,
As fresh and young as morning's gentle light,
How soon 'twill yield unto the common doom,
And darkness gather round thy day so bright.
Unless some art preserve thy vibrant hue,
Or living lines engrave thy sweet renown,
To hold thy essence, ever fresh and new,
When time's rude hand hath cast all beauty down.
So live in verse, where envious age defies,
And shine eternal in admiring eyes.


Lo, in the heaven a fiery chariot speeds,
Bearing the sun from slumber's drowsy hold,
And gilding eastern clouds with golden beads,
A wondrous sight for mortal to behold.
Yet oft that bright orb, 'ere his course be run,
Is veiled by clouds or sudden tempest's frown,
And sets at eve, his daily labour done,
Leaving the world to darkness, still and brown.
So too thy beauty, though it shine so fair,
May face some storm, or shadowed be by woe,
And fleeting joys dissolve into thin air,
As swift as melted flakes of winter snow.
But truth and virtue hold a lasting sway,
And banish sorrow from thy brighter day.


When I behold the ocean's vast expanse,
Whose restless waves forever seek the shore,
Or mark the moon's serene and silent dance,
That pulls the tides and rules the watery roar;
Then think I on the fleeting state of man,
Whose brief existence passes like a dream,
A brittle vessel, built on nature's plan,
Borne on life's ever-swift and changing stream.
For what is strength, or honour, or great fame,
Against the winds of time's remorseless flight?
They are but whispers, an ephemeral name,
Extinguished swiftly in the coming night.
Yet love, alone, defies the common lot,
And lives enduring, though all else be rot.


The gentle comfort of thy loving eye,
Doth chase away the shadows of my fear,
And when I feel that sorrow lingers nigh,
Thy steadfast gaze doth bid my spirit cheer.
Like morning dew upon a drooping flower,
Thy sweet affection lifts my heavy soul,
And lends me strength to face each trying hour,
Making my broken, wounded spirit whole.
What solace then, could greater fortune bring,
Than to possess a treasure such as thine?
A constant spring, from which new hopes do spring,
A sacred bond, eternally divine.
So let my heart, enslaved by thy dear sight,
Forever dwell within thy loving light.


My verse, too plain, doth want a richer dress,
To praise thy worth, which doth my skill exceed,
For words fall short, however I express,
The boundless grace and truth thou dost indeed.
My halting lines cannot thy beauty show,
Nor paint thy virtues with a worthy hand,
But as a faint reflection, pale and low,
They strive to tell what few can understand.
Perchance hereafter, when my dust is laid,
And bolder bards shall seize the tuneful lyre,
Some richer genius, mightier arrayed,
Shall set thy praises to a nobler fire.
Till then, accept these labours, poor and slight,
That fain would capture thee in deathless light.


That I do love thee, let no man deny,
For in my heart thou holdest sovereign sway,
And every thought that from my soul doth fly,
Doth bear thy name unto the coming day.
Though other beauties may their charms display,
And fleeting glances tempt my wandering gaze,
My faithful spirit never strays away,
But keeps its fealty through all life's maze.
For constancy is rooted deep within,
A sturdy oak, that storms cannot defeat,
And loyalty, a virtue free from sin,
Makes every bitter hardship truly sweet.
So trust my truth, as sun doth follow moon,
My love for thee shall never wane too soon.


The fleeting hours, with silent, winged speed,
Do steal away the glory of the morn,
And life's brief lease, a fragile, tender weed,
Is plucked by time ere harvest can be born.
When I reflect upon this hasty flight,
And see how youth doth wither to decay,
How beauty fades and loses all its light,
And strength gives way to weakness, day by day.
Then do I strive to seize each moment fast,
And fill its measure with a worthy deed,
Lest life's rich promise be too quickly past,
And barren plains be found where blossoms bleed.
For in this brief sojourn, 'tis wise and true,
To pluck the bloom, ere winter's icy hue.


If ever soul on earth knew perfect woe,
Or heart did ache with sorrow's sharpest dart,
Then surely I, whose grief doth overflow,
Have felt the pangs that tear a loving heart.
For cruel absence, with its chilling breath,
Hath snatched thee from my fond and eager sight,
And left my days to mourn a living death,
And clothed my spirit in eternal night.
No solace can my weary mind appease,
No fleeting joy can ease this heavy pain,
But thoughts of thee, that drift upon the breeze,
Do bring thy cherished image back again.
So I do wait, with patience calm and deep,
Till fate shall grant the vows that true loves keep.


The fickle world, with all its vain delight,
Doth tempt the soul from paths of steadfast truth,
And draws the unwary into endless night,
Consuming passion, joy, and tender youth.
How many fall before its painted shows,
Its hollow promises and gilded lures,
And reap the bitter fruit of thorny woes,
While virtue's gentle guiding light obscures.
But let not folly lead thy steps astray,
Nor seek renown in riches or in power,
For these shall vanish with the passing day,
As fading blossoms of a summer hour.
Embrace the good, and hold to grace divine,
For lasting peace and lasting joys are thine.


When that I look upon the starry sky,
And mark the planets in their measured dance,
Or trace the comets swiftly passing by,
Whose fiery tails gleam with a fleeting glance;
Then think I on the vastness of our fate,
How small we are within the boundless span,
And how our lives, however grand and great,
Are but a moment in creation's plan.
What then is man, that he should dream of sway,
Or seek to conquer time with earthly might?
His little reign doth vanish in a day,
Consumed by darkness of eternal night.
Yet love alone, a beacon ever bright,
Can pierce the gloom and lead us to the light.


Let others praise the beauty of the rose,
Or sing the lily's pure and spotless grace,
Or marvel how the gentle violet grows,
And lends sweet fragrance to its secret place.
My heart beholds a beauty far more true,
A loveliness that blossoms from within,
A spirit pure, whose goodness shines anew,
And drives away the shadows of all sin.
For outward shows, though pleasing to the eye,
Are but a mask that time can soon deface,
And fleeting charms will vanish and will die,
Leaving no trace of their once fair embrace.
But inward worth, a treasure to behold,
Grows ever brighter, as the days unfold.


How oft my heart, oppressed with worldly care,
Doth seek a refuge from the strife and noise,
And findeth comfort in the silent air,
Amidst the solace of life's simpler joys.
Not in the halls where gilded pomp resides,
Nor in the chase of honour's fleeting gleam,
But where tranquility serenely glides,
As calm reflections in a peaceful stream.
A quiet walk beneath the ancient trees,
A moment's thought, a book of worthy lore,
The gentle murmur of the evening breeze,
These are the riches I do most adore.
For in such peace, my spirit finds its rest,
And banishes the turmoil from my breast.


My love shall live, though kingdoms fall to dust,
And monuments of brass themselves decay,
For in these lines, a faithful, sacred trust
Shall keep thy memory from Time's foul sway.
When marble tombs are worn by wind and rain,
And gilded statues crumble to the ground,
Thy gentle name shall brightly still remain,
In every verse, eternally renowned.
Nor shall grim Death, with all his cruel sting,
Obscure thy worth from future ages' view,
But in this measured song, thy praise I sing,
To make thy beauty ever fresh and new.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


O, never say that I was false of heart,
Though absence seemed my flame to cool and chill,
But think that self-same heaven was my part,
Where thy bright star doth guide my wand'ring will.
I know the subtle changes of the moon,
And how the seasons shift from spring to fall,
Yet in my breast, thy image holds its noon,
And stands unchanging, answering duty's call.
No tide of fortune, no tempestuous blast,
No envious tongue can ever move my mind,
For love like mine was made to ever last,
And seeks no new, when it the best doth find.
Then doubt not, though my steps afar may roam,
My truest heart still finds with thee its home.


When that I was and you were in your prime,
When youth and folly made us both so bold,
Ere wrinkle came, or fell diseases' rhyme,
And story's breath was fresher to be told;
Then did we sport beneath a summer's tree,
And count the stars that glimmered in the night,
Believing then that time would ever flee,
And never bring an end to our delight.
But now the shadows lengthen on the grass,
And colder winds begin to stir the air,
The mirrored glass reflects how moments pass,
And shows the traces of consuming care.
Yet in my heart, the olden flame doth burn,
And for thy love, my spirit still doth yearn.


My love is as a star, whose steady light
Doth guide my vessel on the stormy main;
Though tempest rage, and darkness shroud the night,
Her constant beam doth banish every pain.
No shifting gale can move her fixed intent,
Nor fleeting clouds obscure her visage bright;
On faith's firm rock her precious soul is bent,
And in her truth I find my sole delight.
Let envious tongues pronounce her worth to cease,
Let slander's breath defile her spotless name;
My heart holds fast, and finds in her its peace,
Unshaken by the world's malicious blame.
For she is all that virtue can define,
A sacred love, eternally divine.


When I behold the fading of the rose,
Which flaunts its beauty in the morning sun,
Then droops its head, as twilight softly grows,
Its brief and lovely pilgrimage now done;
I think upon the swiftly passing hours,
How youth and prime do vanish from our sight,
And how the strength of unassailed powers
Is swallowed by oblivion's dark night.
Then do I praise thy beauty, rare and true,
Which holds its sway against time's cruel decree,
And wish these verses might prolong for you
The grace that mortal eyes may cease to see.
So shall thy merit live, beyond compare,
Preserved by art from death's engulfing snare.


Full many a thought doth stir within my breast,
When I consider all that life bestows;
From morning's light, to evening's peaceful rest,
And how each moment swiftly comes and goes.
The pride of kings, the wisdom of the sage,
The merchant's wealth, the soldier's martial might,
All fade at last upon time's cruel stage,
Consuming darkness swallowing the light.
Yet in thy grace, a solace I do find,
A steadfast haven from the worldly strife;
A comfort for my troubled, weary mind,
A promise of a more enduring life.
For while this frame decays, and breath doth part,
Thy image dwells eternal in my heart.


When nature's art doth paint the lily white,
And crimson blush upon the rose impart,
I see the wonders of her glorious might,
But praise thy beauty, which transcends all art.
No flower that blooms, however fair its grace,
Can rival hues that in thy features blend;
No morning star, that lights the ethereal space,
Can with thy radiant countenance contend.
For in thy eyes, a heaven I behold,
Where virtue dwells, and truth doth make its shrine;
A story that no language can unfold,
A love that speaks of something more divine.
So let the world admire its fading show,
While in my heart, thy deathless beauties grow.


My verse is poor, unworthy of thy praise,
And cannot paint thy merit as it ought;
It struggles with a thousand humble ways,
To capture beauty, which no skill hath caught.
For when I strive to make thy virtues clear,
My halting words do falter and grow weak,
And all my efforts fill my soul with fear,
Lest my rude lines thy perfect grace bespeak.
Yet know, dear friend, that though my pen may fail,
And lack the power to do thy honour right,
My heart's true feeling shall at last prevail,
And hold thy image ever in its light.
So take this offering, though it be but small,
My love for thee, which doth surpass them all.


When sorrow's cloud doth overcast my mind,
And bitter thoughts within my spirit dwell,
No earthly comfort can I hope to find,
To break the bondage of this gloomy spell.
I wander forth, beneath the silent moon,
And seek a solace in the midnight air;
But even stars withhold their kindly boon,
And mock my heart, consumed by deep despair.
Then memory recalls thy gentle face,
Thy soothing words, that banish every fear;
And suddenly, a warmth doth fill this place,
And all the shadows swiftly disappear.
Thus art thou heaven, sent from realms above,
To light my darkness with thy tender love.


What bitter pangs do seize my trembling soul,
When I consider Time's relentless sway!
How youth and vigour yield to his control,
And strength and beauty softly melt away.
The vibrant flush that colours childhood's cheek,
The shining eye, that speaks of joy and mirth,
Grow dim and pale, as seasons onward seek,
And claim their tribute from the yielding earth.
Then do I long for some enduring art,
To hold thy grace beyond the common doom;
To seal thy image deep within my heart,
And shield thy beauty from the hungry tomb.
So in these lines, thy gentle light shall shine,
And bid defiance to all things malign.


My thoughts are restless, like a troubled sea,
When absence keeps thy presence from my view;
No peace I find, no solace comes to me,
Until thy gentle image I renew.
Each passing hour doth seem a tedious age,
Each moment stretched beyond its rightful span;
And sorrow's hand doth turn life's joyful page,
To scenes of bleakness for a lonely man.
I count the minutes, measure every day,
And sigh for moments that refuse to haste;
Longing to chase these dreary clouds away,
And know again thy love, I may not waste.
Come swiftly, love, and banish all my pain,
And bring my weary spirit joy again.


The wisdom of the ancients, grave and deep,
Hath taught mankind of life's uncertain stay;
How fleeting joys their brief communion keep,
Then swiftly fade, like shadows of the day.
The strongest fort, the tower built so high,
At length must yield to time's eroding hand;
The mightiest empire, 'neath the spacious sky,
At last will crumble, and forgotten stand.
But love, true love, a firmer fabric weaves,
Than stone or brass, or glory's transient gleam;
Its root is deep, its fruit the heart receives,
A waking truth, and not a fading dream.
So let all else succumb to slow decay,
Our bond shall mock the passing of the day.


When in the throes of sadness I do pine,
And heavy burdens weigh upon my soul,
I think of thee, and then my spirit's shrine
Is filled with comfort, making my heart whole.
For thy dear name, a balm to every wound,
Doth charm away the shadows from my sight;
And in thy memory, my peace is found,
A guiding star, throughout the darkest night.
Let others seek their pleasure in the chase
Of worldly honours, or of fleeting gold;
My joy is fixed upon thy gentle grace,
A treasure richer far than can be told.
So blessèd be the hour when first we met,
Thy love's sweet solace, I shall ne'er forget.


When I do gaze upon thy youthful light,
And mark the fading of each tender grace,
I mourn to think how time with cruelest might,
Shall mar the beauty of thy gentle face.
The roses sweet that bloom in summer's field,
Must soon give way to winter's icy store,
No lasting treasure can their blossoms yield,
But fade and vanish, to be seen no more.
Yet in my verse, thy memory I keep,
Against the ravage of consuming age,
So shall thy virtues from oblivion creep,
And live forever on this lasting page.
Thus love's true essence doth transcend all art,
And finds its dwelling in a constant heart.

From thine eyes, dear love, a wondrous light,
That doth before my waking spirit stand,
Dispelling shadows of the darkest night,
A guiding star held firmly in thy hand.
No sunbeam could with such pure beauty gleam,
Nor fairer vision ever bless my true
And fondest hope, a sweet and endless dream,
Reflected brightly in my loving view.
What potent magic dost thou cast on me,
That fills each moment with such potent power?
No joy like this could ever bloom for thee,
To light my spirit in its darkest hour.
So in my heart thy image I shall find,
The dearest treasure to my constant mind.

The worldly goods that mortal men do hold,
Are but a lease that hastens to decay;
No lasting solace can their worth unfold,
They vanish swift, as shadows from the day.
What profit hath the man whom riches steal,
When all his golden moments softly fade?
Before grim death, all earthly powers kneel,
No lasting fortress by his wealth is made.
Then why should man his fleeting wealth pursue,
When bitter anguish is its final woe?
Let virtue guide his spirit, firm and true,
For transient glories do but falsely show.
Hold not thy riches with a grip so fast,
For only virtue doth forever last.

The fleeting beauty of a tender age,
Doth quickly pass, a transient, fading show;
And time, the churl, doth turn each youthful page,
To quench the spark that made thy spirit glow.
No earthly bloom can stand against his might,
Nor golden treasure can his grasp withstand,
But all must yield unto eternal night,
And vanish swift, like dust within the hand.
Yet shall my verse thy dearest image give,
A lasting monument, beyond all breath,
Where through my lines thy loveliness shall live,
Defying ruin, and the sting of death.
So shall thy form still gladden every eye,
And conquer all that seeks to bring thee nigh.

Though envious tongues with venomous spite may rage,
And slander seek to wound my spirit true,
My steadfast love, though time doth turn each page,
Shall ever hold thee in its constant view.
Let seasons change, and summer's warmth decrease,
And winter's frost o'er nature's stillness creep,
My firm devotion brings my spirit peace,
As silent stars their nightly watches keep.
For I have vowed my faith, and it shall stand
Against all woes that seek to tear apart,
No foreign shore, nor any distant land,
Can break the bonds that link my loyal heart.
No earthly power can divide my soul,
For thou and I art fashioned to be whole.

Behold the sun, when in its youthful light,
It doth ascend and paint the morn with gold,
And draws all eyes, a marvel to the sight,
A kingly progress, glorious to behold.
But when its journey hath its zenith passed,
And dims the glory of its morning bright,
Its fading beams no longer hold so fast,
As shadows deepen into coming night.
So thou, whose beauty doth now charm each eye,
And drawest homage from each gazing mind,
Shalt feel the stealthy hand of time draw nigh,
And leave thy youthful splendor far behind.
Then wisely use this fleeting prime of thine,
Lest fading moments cause thy light to pine.

Let fickle Fortune shift her painted scene,
And turn her wheel with ever-changing hand,
Let storms arise where skies were once serene,
And scatter blessings o'er a barren land.
My constant spirit shall not feel her sway,
Nor shall her tempests move my firm intent,
For in thy love, I find my guiding day,
A solace sweet, from heaven gently sent.
Though worldly goods may crumble to the dust,
And fading glories vanish from the sight,
In thy true heart, my steadfast soul doth trust,
A beacon shining through the darkest night.
So let the world around me ebb and flow,
My love for thee shall never cease to grow.

When icicles hang down and winds do blow,
And all the fields are clad in hoary white,
No tender flower doth on the meadow grow,
And nature sleeps through many a cheerless night.
The summer's bloom and autumn's fleeting grace,
Are vanished quite, their colours flown away;
No verdant leaf upon the bough doth trace
The memory of a warmer, brighter day.
Yet in this bleakness, hope doth softly creep,
A promise whispered on the northern breeze,
That 'neath the snow, where seeds in silence sleep,
New life shall rise and banish all disease.
For spring shall come, with sunshine bright and grand,
And bring new beauty to the waiting land.

As plants do lean upon the sun's warm light,
To draw their sustenance from golden ray,
So doth my spirit, through the darkest night,
Depend upon thy presence, night and day.
When thou art absent, all my joys are flown,
And heavy gloom doth fill my weary mind,
No greater sadness have I ever known,
Nor comfort else can my poor spirit find.
Then let no shadow dim thy radiance clear,
Nor cruel distance hold thee far apart,
But ever be, my comfort, ever near,
To cheer the dwelling of my longing heart.
For without thee, my life doth lose its goal,
Thou art the anchor of my very soul.

Not from the stars do I my judgment pluck,
Nor can I tell of fortunes good or ill,
I have no skill to read what fate hath stuck
Upon the wheel, to work its wondrous will.
But from thy virtues, which do shine so clear,
I draw my prescience, of a happier state,
That truth and goodness banish every fear,
And overcome the frowns of cruelest fate.
For though thy beauty fade, as all things must,
And time's cold hand doth mar thy youthful face,
Thy constant spirit, free from worldly rust,
Shall hold its glory, in its destined place.
So shalt thou live, when outward charms are past,
For only virtue doth forever last.

When all the world doth turn from thee away,
And fleeting friendship proves a hollow sound,
My loyal spirit shall forever stay,
Where true devotion ever can be found.
Though envious time may steal thy youthful grace,
And mar the beauty of thy tender face,
My steadfast verse shall hold thy treasured place,
And keep thy memory safe from all disgrace.
For while I live, and breath doth fill my frame,
Thy cherished image in my soul shall dwell,
And every line shall echo forth thy name,
A loving truth, that time can never quell.
So in these rhymes, thy essence I impart,
The deathless treasure of my constant heart.

The fleeting moments of our mortal day,
Are but a shadow, transient and brief;
They swiftly pass, and vanish quite away,
Like autumn's glory, or a summer's leaf.
Then let us not in idle slumber lie,
Nor waste the riches of our precious youth,
But seize the joys that swiftly pass us by,
And fill our spirits with life's vibrant truth.
For when old age with frosty hand doth creep,
And steals the vigor from our failing frame,
Then shall we find that regrets gather deep,
And naught remains but sorrow and dull shame.
Live then thy life with passion, bold and fast,
For every moment quickly hastens past.

Those gentle eyes, that shine with inward grace,
Are windows to a soul both pure and deep,
Reflecting kindness from their tender space,
Where tender thoughts in silent vigil keep.
No hidden malice doth within them dwell,
Nor any falsehood cloud their honest gleam,
But crystal truth, a story they do tell,
More fair than waking thought or pleasant dream.
When I behold their calm and quiet light,
My troubled spirit finds its comfort true,
Dispelling shadows of the darkest night,
And making all my weary being new.
So in their depths, my truest self I see,
My dearest love, eternally in thee.

The subtle thief of time doth steal our youth,
And plough deep furrows on the fairest brow,
Consuming beauty, health, and steadfast truth,
As swiftly as the moments vanish now.
What solace then for fragile mortal kind,
Against the ruin that doth all consume?
No balm exists, no refuge we can find,
To 'scape the shadow of a timeless tomb.
Unless, my love, in thee, a power reside,
To stand unconquered 'gainst oblivion's might,
And in this verse, thy spirit shall abide,
To shine eternal, banishing the night.
So shall our bond, defying death's harsh call,
Outlive the ages and transcend them all.


My tongue doth falter when I would commend
The matchless grace that in thy person dwells,
So poor a verse cannot aspire to mend
The silent stories that thy beauty tells.
My words are faint, unworthy of thy sight,
A humble tribute from a troubled breast,
Yet pure affection lends my spirit light,
And sets my fervent labours to the test.
For what I lack in art, I gain in heart,
A true devotion, constant and sincere,
Which plays its fond and unpretending part,
And holds thy image ever shining clear.
So take this gift, though simple it may seem,
A lover's truth, more potent than a dream.


When chill December doth usurp July,
And summer's blossom yields to winter's frost,
When golden leaves from lofty branches fly,
And all the transient beauty then is lost;
So too the world doth change its fickle face,
And fortunes turn with every passing breeze,
No steadfast hope, no firm abiding place,
But swift decay in all that nature sees.
Yet in my heart, one constant fire burns,
A sacred flame that time cannot abate,
To thee, my loyal spirit ever turns,
Defying ruin, destiny, and fate.
For true affection knows no ebb nor flow,
But stronger grows, the more the tempests blow.


How swift the moments pass, how brief our stay,
A fleeting breath upon the ocean's foam,
As blossoms fair that flourish for a day,
Then fade to nothing, finding nature's home.
What art can hold the rose when it is plucked?
Or stay the shadow ere it turns to night?
By time's decree, all earthly things are sucked
Into oblivion, hid from human sight.
But beauty's essence, by a poet's hand,
May live anew in lines of potent verse,
Enduring more than any shifting sand,
Defying death, and time's relentless curse.
So grant thy grace, to make my labours known,
That in these rhymes, thy spirit may be shown.


When doubts assail and shadows fill my mind,
And dark forebodings whisper of despair,
I seek thy image, solace there to find,
A star of hope that banishes all care.
For in thy gaze, a steadfast truth I see,
A loyal spirit, constant and profound,
Which drives away the fears that trouble me,
And heals the deepest, most pernicious wound.
Let tempest rage, and fortunes ebb and flow,
Let envious tongues speak falsehoods to the air,
My heart for thee a changeless faith doth show,
Beyond the reach of sorrow or of snare.
So strong my love, it fears no earthly strife,
The very anchor of my fragile life.


The morning sun, when first it gilds the east,
And paints the sky with hues of rosy light,
Doth hold its beauty, though it soon hath ceased,
To yield to noon, and then to fading night.
No summer's flower, however sweet and rare,
Can boast such grace as in thy form I see,
No moonlit stream, no dawn-besprinkled air,
Can match the mild perfections found in thee.
For thou art fair, beyond compare's embrace,
A living wonder, framed by nature's art,
A radiant marvel, blessing time and place,
Enthroned forever in my loyal heart.
So let the world its lesser beauties show,
Thou art the zenith, whither all must go.


When wanderers are lost upon the main,
And darkling clouds conceal the guiding star,
They yearn for light, to find their course again,
To know what haven lies beyond afar.
So in life's tempest, tossed by every tide,
My weary soul doth often lose its way,
And seeks a steady beacon to abide,
To lead it safely from the troubled day.
That beacon art thou, shining ever bright,
A constant comfort in this mortal strife,
Dispelling shadows with thy gentle light,
The very compass of my errant life.
For whilst thou shin'st, no darkness can prevail,
Thou art the pilot, I the trusting sail.


How like a winter is my soul bereft,
When thy dear presence doth no longer gleam,
And joy itself, no vital spark hath left,
But fades away, as in a vanished dream.
The birds are mute, the flowers droop and die,
The sun itself doth wear a paler hue,
No sweet delight can captivate mine eye,
When I am parted, cherished one, from you.
For absence is a keen and bitter foe,
That steals the colour from the brightest day,
And fills the empty hours with silent woe,
Until thy fond return shall chase away.
Come back, my love, and banish all my pain,
And make my desolate spirit bloom again.


My pen, though humble, labours to express
The boundless reverence my spirit feels,
A deep devotion, born of pure excess,
Which all thy perfect excellence reveals.
For in thy shadow, I would gladly dwell,
Content to serve, and ever to obey,
A loyal subject, bound beneath thy spell,
Through darkest night and brightest shining day.
No worldly honours do I seek to gain,
No fleeting treasures, bought with toil and strife,
But only this: to banish every pain,
And lend some solace to thy precious life.
So take my vows, engraved in lines of truth,
And be the comfort of my fading youth.


When first thine eyes did meet my gazing sight,
A sudden wonder seized my startled breast,
As if a star had fallen from the night,
And found a dwelling-place within my rest.
No common beauty, but a grace profound,
Did then disclose its magic to my soul,
A harmony in every feature found,
To make my fractured spirit true and whole.
From that blest moment, knew I not my name,
But lived enchanted by thy sovereign sway,
And felt within my heart a burning flame,
Which guides my footsteps, lighting up my way.
So was I born anew, by love's sweet art,
And thou thenceforth didst hold my captive heart.


Ah, wherefore did my unavailing tongue
Attempt to utter what the heart concealed?
When no fit praise by mortal can be sung,
To match the beauty that thy face revealed.
My simple words, unworthy of thy name,
But poorly show the fervent zeal I bear,
And in their weakness, bring my soul to shame,
That cannot paint thee fair beyond compare.
For Nature's skill hath wrought thee past all art,
And made thy form a wonder to behold,
Whilst I, with barren craft and failing heart,
Can but a fraction of thy worth unfold.
Forgive, dear love, this unaccomplished strain,
My fault it is, that speaks my love in vain.


All mortal things are subject to decay,
And brief the span allotted to our breath,
The brightest sun must yield to fading day,
And every living creature taste of death.
What hope remains for us, who stand so frail,
Against the universal, common doom?
When joy itself doth quickly turn to wail,
And shadows gather in the silent room.
Yet in the steadfast witness of my heart,
A truth unyielding, ever firm, I find,
That love for thee shall play a deathless part,
And leave all transient earthly woes behind.
So let fate frown, and ruin seize the earth,
My love for thee shall prove its endless worth.


O, lest the world should task you to recite
What merit in my verse at all is found,
Or what it is that makes me write,
When all my best doth to oblivion bound;
You are my all the argument I write,
The field wherein my spirit doth contend,
The constant star that guides my lonely night,
And to my thoughts doth true perfection lend.
And as the moon doth draw the ocean's tide,
So doth thy beauty draw my very soul,
My heart, in thrall, can nowhere else abide,
But yields to thy most absolute control.
So blame me not if I record your praise,
For you alone give substance to my days.


O from what power hast thou this powerful might,
With insufficiency to move my mind
To scorn the fair and count the beauty slight
Of all this world besides, yet bear thy kind?
When I compare my state with thine, I find
No cause but thine to make my heart thus swell;
For by thy grace, my spirit is refined,
And every doubt doth in thy presence quell.
So let me not be false, nor thou unkind,
But let our love endure through every test,
A bond of hearts that time can never bind
To fading woes or troubles that infest.
For in thy love, my truest joy I gain,
And all my sorrows turn to peace again.


When lovely youth doth in its glory bloom,
And nature's hand with cunning art doth grace,
Doth beauty fade, and hasten to its doom,
Ere time's swift foot effaceth every trace?
Ah, cruel question, posed by thoughts of dread,
For fleeting joys do quickly pass away,
And memories of pleasure swiftly fled,
Leave naught behind but sorrow and dismay.
But in these lines, thy virtues I will bind,
A testament against oblivion's sway,
So future ages shall thy beauty find,
And praise thee still, when dust doth thee array.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


My love, no frost shall blight thy tender spring,
Nor envious clouds obscure thy morning's light,
For in my verse, thy deathless praise I sing,
To banish shadow with eternal might.
Let Time, the thief, steal beauty from the rose,
And scatter dust where golden tresses lay,
The steadfast spirit that within thee glows
Shall mock his scythe, and fear no dire decay.
For though the world may turn to ash and dust,
And monuments of kings forgotten lie,
My faithful heart, in which I place my trust,
Shall hold thy image till the heavens sigh.
So rest assured, though earthly glories cease,
Thy name shall live, and find eternal peace.


The morning sun, when from the east it springs,
Doth paint the clouds with hues of fiery gold,
And joyful birds on soaring, feathered wings,
A dulcet, waking melody unfold.
Yet all this pomp, this transient, painted show,
Is but a shadow to thy living grace,
For from thy eyes, a brighter lustre flow,
That doth illuminate this mortal place.
What need have I of jewels, rich and rare,
Or garments spun with thread of finest gleam,
When thy dear presence doth all else compare
To but a faint and unavailing dream?
Thy single glance doth richer wealth impart,
Than all the treasures earth holds in her heart.


When I consider how my days consume,
And fleeting hours like hurried shadows fly,
I fear that soon, encompassed by the tomb,
My mortal form shall unremembered lie.
But then I turn, dear friend, to thoughts of thee,
Whose gentle spirit doth my soul sustain,
And in thy truth, a lasting hope I see,
A balm to soothe my sorrow and my pain.
For though the Fates may sever earthly ties,
And memory fade with each succeeding year,
The bond we share, beneath these changing skies,
Shall banish every doubt and every fear.
Thus shall my spirit find eternal rest,
Enshrined within the chamber of thy breast.


The eye of heaven doth often gaze askance,
Upon the earth, with cloudy, sullen brow,
And changing fortune leads a fickle dance,
To mock the hopes that human hearts avow.
What joy hath man, that doth not swiftly fly?
What treasure gained, that time doth not consume?
All worldly pomp beneath the sun must die,
And sink at last into a silent tomb.
Yet in thy truth, a constant light I find,
A beacon steady 'midst the storm's dark rage,
A lasting comfort to my troubled mind,
A noble worth that doth defy all age.
So let the world its fleeting pleasures spend,
My love for thee shall never know an end.


Lo, in the sky, the sun doth bravely rise,
Dispelling shadows of the fading night,
And with his beams, he opens mortal eyes,
To greet the world with freshness and with light.
So in my soul, when darkness held its sway,
And heavy grief did weigh my spirit down,
Thy presence came, a brighter, fairer day,
And banished sorrow from my weary town.
For in thy smile, a healing power dwells,
To mend the heart that suffered bitter pain,
And from thy lips, a wondrous music wells,
To cheer the soul and make it whole again.
No greater solace can my spirit crave,
Than thy dear love, that doth my being save.


The fickle world, with ever-changing show,
Doth lure men's hearts to seek what cannot last,
And swift desires, like restless waters, flow,
To joys that perish in the fleeting past.
Some court for power, others for mere gain,
And some for praise, that is but empty air,
Their weary lives consumed by toil and pain,
To grasp at shadows, fraught with future care.
But I, dear friend, no longer seek such things,
For in thy truth, a steadfast joy I find,
That brighter shines than crowns of earthly kings,
A lasting comfort to my peaceful mind.
Let fortune frown, and all her gifts withdraw,
Thy constant heart fulfills all nature's law.


Full many a flower, that doth in beauty bloom,
Is born to blush unseen, and waste its scent,
And many a gem, within the ocean's gloom,
Lies hid from view, its brilliance never lent.
So too, perchance, a spirit pure and bright,
May pass through life, unheeded and unknown,
Its virtues veiled, its gentle, inward light,
A lonely secret, cherished all alone.
But thou, whose grace doth shine for all to see,
Art like the sun, that warms the waking earth,
No hidden jewel, but a spreading tree,
Whose generous branches give abundant birth.
Thy truth and beauty, openly displayed,
Have made my heart eternally remade.


The heavy clouds that weep their dreary rain,
Do hide the heavens from our longing sight,
And sullen winds sweep o'er the barren plain,
To shroud the world in sorrow and in night.
So too, my soul, when distant from thy face,
Doth sink beneath a weight of grievous woe,
And finds no comfort in this joyless place,
Where former pleasures can no longer glow.
Oh, haste thee back, and banish all my fears,
And let thy presence chase away the gloom,
For only thou canst dry these bitter tears,
And rescue me from this despairing tomb.
Return, my light, and make the darkness flee,
For all my solace doth reside in thee.


When first I saw thee, youth had set its seal
Upon thy brow, with beauty's tender grace,
And time, the foe, had not begun to steal
The budding promise from thy lovely face.
Now years have passed, and subtly left their mark,
With silver strands among thy raven hair,
And wisdom's lines appear amidst the dark,
A testament to life's incessant care.
Yet in thine eyes, a deeper lustre dwells,
Than ever shone in youth's tempestuous pride,
A richer beauty, that the heart compels,
A spirit true, where gentle virtues hide.
So fear not age, nor what the seasons bring,
Thy worth eternal, I shall ever sing.


The proudest towers, that pierce the azure sky,
And ancient walls, where warlike banners flew,
Must yield at last, and in oblivion lie,
Their strength forgotten, and their glories few.
No earthly might can Time's sharp scythe withstand,
Nor beauty keep its bloom against his rage,
He lays his hand on all throughout the land,
And turns to dust each fleeting, gilded page.
But in my verse, thy beauty I shall bind,
A lasting challenge to his cruel decree,
So future ages shall thy virtues find,
And marvel at the deathless grace of thee.
Though stones may crumble, and though empires fall,
My loving lines shall triumph over all.


Let fickle fate her bitter wrath impart,
And storm of sorrow beat upon my head,
Let treach'rous lies attempt to break my heart,
And envious tongues pronounce my spirit dead.
I shall not yield, nor let my courage fail,
While in my soul thy noble image gleams,
For thy true love, a shield that must prevail,
Doth guard me from all dark and fearful dreams.
What power hath pain, what sting hath harsh regret,
When thy dear presence doth my spirit raise?
All worldly troubles I can well forget,
Lost in the wonder of thy loving gaze.
So come what may, I stand in strength complete,
Thy steadfast love makes every sorrow sweet.


My love is like a star, both fixed and bright,
Whose steady beams throughout the darkness stream;
It doth not change with day, nor yield to night,
But holds its course, a constant, guiding gleam.
Though envious clouds may seek to dim its ray,
And fortune's tempest rage with bitter scorn,
My loyal heart shall never turn away,
But faithful stand, as on creation's morn.
Let fleeting pleasures tempt the fickle mind,
And vows be broken with a careless word;
In constancy alone shall true love find
Its perfect peace, by righteous Heaven heard.
For while I breathe, and while my blood doth flow,
My steadfast love for thee shall ever grow.


When in the glass I see my wrinkles creep,
And youth's fair blossoms turn to winter's frost,
A sudden tremor through my soul doth sweep,
To think how much by time hath been quite lost.
My fading locks, once dark as raven's wing,
Are now arrayed in silver, pale and grey;
The joyful songs that once my spirit sing,
Are muffled now by life's declining day.
Yet do I find some solace in this plight,
That though my body wastes and doth decay,
My inward spirit gleams with clearer light,
And doth perceive a more enduring way.
For beauty fades, and earthly pleasures cease,
But truth and love can bring the soul release.


Why doth my verse so seldom show thy worth,
Though all my heart doth worship at thy shrine?
Is't that my spirit finds no words on earth
To match the grace that doth in thee combine?
My tongue, alas, seems fettered by its fear,
Lest praise too faint should do thy beauty wrong;
No gilded phrases make thy virtues clear,
Nor can my humble measure hold thy song.
Others may boast of passion's fervent flame,
And paint their loves with colours bold and bright;
I, silently, but whisper forth thy name,
And find my solace in thy gentle light.
For true affection needs no loud display,
But shines most pure when words are put away.


When first I saw thy eyes, a heaven did seem
To break upon my dull and weary gaze;
As from a slumber, wakened by a dream,
I felt a warmth that banished all my daze.
Thy gentle voice, a music soft and low,
Did steal into my soul with sweet accord,
And made my captive heart sincerely know
That I had found my cherished, rightful lord.
The world without, with all its harsh alarms,
Did fade away, and left but thee and I;
Enfolded safe within thy loving arms,
I wished that moment never more to die.
For in thy presence, all my sorrows cease,
And troubled thoughts are hushed to perfect peace.


The weary sun descends to ocean's bed,
And draws a veil of darkness o'er the land;
The world is hushed, as if in slumber led,
And lonely thoughts begin to take command.
Then in my mind, thy image doth arise,
More bright than moon, more radiant than star;
It doth disperse the shadows from my eyes,
And brings thy presence, though thou art afar.
What solace then, to know that thou art true,
And though removed by space, yet ever near;
My heart finds comfort in this tender view,
And banishes each doubt, each rising fear.
So shall I dream until the morn doth break,
And for thy love, each silent night partake.


Let those who boast of titles, wealth, or power,
Exult in trifles, fading with the light;
I count my treasure in a single hour
Spent in thy company, both day and night.
No kingly crown, no scepter's proud command,
No vast dominion stretched across the sea,
Can yield such joy within this mortal land,
As one sweet moment I do spend with thee.
For outward pomp and fortune's fickle smile
Are but a shadow, transient and vain;
They may deceive the foolish for a while,
But cannot soothe the heart's enduring pain.
My true content, my soul's profound delight,
Is found in thee, my never-setting light.


When nature's cunning hand hath wrought its best,
And painted flowers with hues beyond compare,
Or set the lark's sweet music in my breast,
To fill the morning with a joyful air;
Then do I think upon thy matchless grace,
And how all beauty doth in thee reside,
No mortal art can mirror forth thy face,
Nor all the world such perfect form can hide.
For thou art fashioned from celestial mold,
A vision sent from heavens high above;
A story that can never be untold,
The living essence of eternal love.
So let the world display its fleeting show,
My heart sees naught but where thy beauties glow.


If I could choose what fate should mark my way,
Or bid the stars decree my earthly end,
I would but ask to see thee every day,
And on thy gentle counsel still depend.
No grand ambition stirs my tranquil breast,
No lust for power, nor for worldly fame;
In thy affection I have found my rest,
And cherish only love's enduring flame.
Let others chase the phantom joys of earth,
And build their fortunes on deceit and guile;
My humble lot, from my true love's sweet birth,
Is but to serve thee for a little while.
And when my breath at last begins to fail,
To whisper forth thy name, beyond the veil.


The sun itself, though sovereign in the sky,
Doth sometimes veil its golden, glorious face;
And earthly kings, how proud soe'er they lie,
Are often humbled from their lofty place.
So human beauty, though it shine so bright,
Is but a fleeting flower, quickly gone;
And fortune's favours, changing with the light,
Are oft withdrawn ere rising of the dawn.
But in thy spirit, truth and grace reside,
A beauty deeper than the outward show;
Which time's relentless current cannot ride,
Nor envious shadows from its essence throw.
Thus, though all else to rust and ruin fall,
Thy inner worth shall stand, surviving all.


My thoughts, like weary pilgrims, journey far,
To seek thy image in each waking dream;
They follow thee, as sailors seek a star,
Or thirsty travellers a cooling stream.
And when they find thee, though but in my mind,
A sudden sweetness doth my soul imbue;
All worldly cares I leave then far behind,
And dwell awhile in joys both fresh and new.
But oh, what sorrow when the vision fades,
And stark reality resumes its reign!
My spirit then in mournful darkness wades,
And longs for that sweet imagery again.
Thus, caught between what is and what might be,
I live enslaved to absent thoughts of thee.


The world, with all its bustling, noisy strife,
And busy cares that vex the human breast,
Doth oft encroach upon my inner life,
And rob my weary spirit of its rest.
But then I turn my thoughts to thee, my dear,
And instantly, the tumult fades away;
Thy gentle image brings a solace clear,
And drives the shadows from my troubled day.
As storms subside before the sun's bright ray,
So vanish worries when thy face I see;
Thou art the anchor in life's stormy fray,
The constant comfort that upholds my plea.
So let me keep thy essence ever near,
To banish sorrow, banish every fear.


No greater love than this can e'er be told,
Than that which binds my fervent heart to thine;
A treasure richer far than gems or gold,
A sacred bond, enduring and divine.
Though time may steal the freshness from thy cheek,
And winter's frost upon thy brow appear,
My steadfast soul shall never cease to seek
The deeper beauty that I hold so dear.
For outward form doth quickly fade away,
And earthly pleasures vanish in the dust;
But loyal love, defying all decay,
Holds firm its truth, in everlasting trust.
So let us journey through life's changing scene,
With hearts entwined, eternally serene.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


O, call not me to justify the wrong
That thy unkindness lays upon my heart;
To excuse the breach of faith, however strong
My love for thee, from which I cannot part.
For I must bear the cross of thy disdain,
And suffer patiently thy sharpest dart,
While in my soul a constant, bitter pain
Doth tear my very being quite apart.
Yet in this torment, I will not accuse
Thy fickle nature, nor thy cruel caprice,
But rather blame the love I cannot lose,
Which binds my spirit and denies me peace.
Thus, though thou wound me with each passing day,
My foolish heart still wishes thee to stay.


Let not my love be called inconstant still,
Though outward shows may shift with every wind,
For in my heart, I hold thy perfect will,
A steadfast truth that no decay can find.
The world may turn, and seasons come and go,
And fickle fortune change her painted face,
But my devotion doth no variance know,
Fixed as a star within its destined space.
For what is love, if not a constant flame,
That burns undimmed by time's consuming fire?
It scorns the whisper of a fickle name,
And lifts the soul to heaven's high desire.
So rest assured, my faith shall never swerve,
While life and breath within this body serve.


Shall I compare thee to a winter's morn?
Too cold and stark thou art, devoid of cheer.
More like the blush when rosy day is born,
Or April's promise, banishing all fear.
But ah, the sun too oft its face doth hide,
And transient clouds eclipse the golden ray,
And every bloom that opens in its pride,
By chance or nature's course must fade away.
Thy beauty, though, shall know no swift decline,
Nor lose the grace that Time doth now bestow,
For in these lines, a deathless art shall shine,
Against the storms that earthly currents flow.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


How like a dream before the waking eye,
Do pleasures fade, and joys take swiftest flight!
The dearest moments vanish in a sigh,
And leave the soul to dwell in lonely night.
So do I find my truest solace gone,
When from thy presence I am forced to part,
And in the absence, mourn the rising dawn,
That brings no comfort to my longing heart.
For as the sun withdraws its genial gleam,
And shadows lengthen on the weary land,
So doth my spirit, lost in sorrow's stream,
Await the touch of thy beloved hand.
Return, my light, and chase away the gloom,
Ere I am plunged into a timeless tomb.


Some say that love is but a summer's sport,
A fleeting fancy born of youthful fire,
That with the fading season doth cut short
Its ardent flame and passion's fond desire.
But such a love is not the love I know,
Nor such the bond that binds my heart to thine;
For true affection doth forever grow,
And deepens with each passing, altering sign.
It is a rock 'gainst which the ocean beats,
Unmoved, unyielding, steadfast through the storm,
A holy warmth that bitter cold defeats,
And keeps the spirit ever true and warm.
So let them speak of love that soon must die,
My constant heart shall prove their words a lie.


Methinks no words can tell my heart's true state,
When thou art absent, and my soul's forlorn;
Each moment seems an age, so slow and great
The sorrow that within my breast is born.
The sun itself, though it doth brightly shine,
Appears but dim, without thy living grace;
The sweetest music holds no charm for mine,
When I behold not thy beloved face.
For thou art all the comfort I can claim,
The very essence of my waking dream,
And absence doth but fan the tender flame,
And make thee dearer than thou erst did seem.
So haste thy steps, and banish all my woe,
And let thy presence make my spirit glow.


O, how much more doth beauty beauteous seem
By that sweet ornament which truth doth give!
The rose looks fair, but fairer we it deem
For that sweet odour which doth in it live.
The canker blooms have full as deep a dye
As the perfumed tincture of the roses,
Hang on the thorn when summer's breath goes by,
But for their virtue only us disposes.
The fragrant flowers, when their leaves are shed,
Do leave their virtues in a sweet still'd treasure;
When summer's breath their masked buds hath fed,
They die in show, but live in sweet measure.
So are those beauties that do live with grace,
When death's keen frost shall taint all lovely face.


When wasteful time with envious hand doth seize
The tender prime of youth, and makes it fade,
And bitter winter steals fair summer's ease,
Upon thy brows a cruel mark is made.
The blooming rose doth swiftly lose its hue,
Its fragrant breath, which once did charm the air,
So beauty's lease is ever brief and new,
And finds no stay from ruin or from care.
Yet shalt thou live, though ages roll and fly,
In deathless lines, by sacred verse designed,
When marble statues crumbling silent lie,
A truer monument thy worth shall find.
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.


My love is like the sun, which doth ascend
Each morn anew, and brings the light of day,
And though the day must find its weary end,
Its golden promise doth not pass away.
No clouded thoughts can dim thy spirit's grace,
No stormy gale can shake thy steadfast mind,
For in thy heart, a truer dwelling place
Than mortal change, my constant soul doth find.
Let fickle fortune frown upon my plea,
Or envious tongues with malice seek to sting,
My firm devotion shall remain with thee,
As birds in spring their joyful carols sing.
For in thy gaze, my dearest hopes reside,
And by thy truth, my spirit is supplied.


My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damask'd, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground.
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.


Adornèd night, thou cloak of gentle sleep,
Whose sable mantle doth the world enfold,
Thou dost from weary hearts their sorrows keep,
And tales of quiet slumber hast untold.
When Phoebus sinks beneath the western wave,
And weary mortals seek their soft repose,
Thou dost from toil and waking torment save,
And bindest eyelids where sweet slumber grows.
Oh, let thy balm upon my spirit fall,
And charm to peace the troubles of the day,
That dreams may come, responsive to thy call,
And bear my soul to regions far away.
So shall my mind in peaceful stillness dwell,
Until the morn bids thee a fond farewell.


When sorrow's cloud o'ershadows my poor heart,
And thoughts of gloom within my spirit breed,
I seek a solace, a most gentle art,
To cure the wounds which bitter moments feed.
Then to thy face, my mind doth swiftly fly,
And in thy gaze, a healing balm I find,
For in those depths, no darker shadows lie,
But peace and grace, to soothe my troubled mind.
So by thy image, I am oft restored,
From melancholy's cold and damp embrace,
And by thy sweet remembrance am adored,
E'en though I yearn for thy beloved face.
Thus absence keen, though pain it doth impart,
Cannot unfix the love within my heart.


The gentle wind, which through the branches plays,
And whispers secrets to the rustling leaves,
Doth bear thy scent through all the verdant ways,
A phantom sweetness that my sense perceives.
The blushing dawn, which paints the eastern skies,
Reflects the glow that from thy cheek doth flow,
And in the stars, the brightness of thine eyes,
A silent promise that my heart doth know.
Each bird that sings its carol to the day,
Doth echo notes of music in thy voice,
And every bloom that opens on the spray,
Makes my fond spirit inwardly rejoice.
For all fair things that nature can impart,
Are but faint copies of thy living art.


My love to thee is as the ocean deep,
Which constant flows, and knows no ebbing tide,
A boundless wonder, secrets it doth keep,
Where all my hopes and tender thoughts reside.
No storm can fright its calm and placid breast,
No changing winds can turn its steady course,
But ever onward, by its truth possessed,
It draws its essence from a vital source.
Let other loves, like brooks, run dry and fade,
Or transient as the morning's fickle dew,
My deep affection shall remain unswayed,
Forever strong, forever pure and true.
For in thy heart, my anchor I have cast,
And know that love like ours is built to last.


My heavy heart, with sorrow doth contend,
When thou art absent, and my spirits pine;
No joy I find, nor solace doth portend,
But dark despair, where'er my steps incline.
As weary traveller longs for journey's end,
Or parched earth doth for gentle rain complain,
So doth my soul for thy dear presence send,
And count each hour a season of deep pain.
For in thy look, my comfort doth reside,
Thy voice alone can banish all my fear,
And though the world may mock where true loves hide,
Thy memory keeps my fragile essence near.
Oh, haste thee back, and quell this bitter smart,
And with thy light, restore my drooping heart.


The fleeting world doth teach us sorrow's sting,
As summer's green gives way to autumn's gold,
And joyful birds that erst did sweetly sing,
Are silenced by the winter's bitter cold.
The rose's blush, that graced the early morn,
By noon doth droop and scatter on the ground;
Thus every mortal thing to death is born,
And beauty's lease but for a while is found.
So too our days, swift as a shadow pass,
And all our glories hasten to their doom,
Reflected briefly in a fragile glass,
Before we vanish to the silent tomb.
Then let us love, whilst yet we have our breath,
Ere envious Time doth seal our eyes in death.


Mine eye doth watch, what time thy form is far,
And grieves to know how distance doth divide;
As mariners who seek a guiding star,
So doth my soul for thy return abide.
No fleeting fancy holds my heart in thrall,
Nor sudden passion doth my senses bind;
But constant truth, that answers nature's call,
A love sincere, of truest, noblest kind.
Let changeful winds blow foul or fair above,
Let worldly trials vex my mortal state,
My steadfast faith, and everlasting love,
Shall hold thee dear, despite opposing fate.
For come what may, or fortune's harsh decree,
My truest self doth ever rest with thee.


My thoughts are not so fair as they should be,
Nor doth my tongue the proper praises frame;
When I would speak the worth I find in thee,
My humble words but put thy grace to shame.
For how can language, built of common clay,
Describe the light that doth from thee proceed?
As well might darkness strive to conquer day,
Or barren ground bring forth a fruitful seed.
I lack the skill, the eloquence, the art,
To paint thy virtues in their true array;
My mind's invention plays a feeble part,
And doth but tarnish what it would display.
Then pardon me, if silence be my plea,
For what I feel, no words can speak of thee.


Where are the hours of sweet and gentle youth,
That danced like fawns upon the morning dew?
And where the vows of everlasting truth,
That once my ardent spirit did imbue?
Alas, they're fled, like shadows on the plain,
Chased by the sun, or scattered by the breeze;
And in their place, a sorrow doth remain,
A quiet ache, that finds no heart-felt ease.
For time, with cruel and unrelenting hand,
Doth steal our vigour, and our beauty blight,
Leaving but memories in a barren land,
To mock the brilliance of a vanished light.
Then let me mourn what joy did once possess,
And find some solace in my heart's distress.


Thy presence shines, a beacon in the night,
To guide my vessel through life's stormy seas;
Thy gentle smile doth fill my soul with light,
And banish all my sorrows and unease.
No greater joy than to behold thy face,
No sweeter music than to hear thy voice;
Thy every motion, filled with charming grace,
Doth make my weary spirit to rejoice.
As thirsty flowers long for morning dew,
Or parched lands crave the solace of the rain,
So doth my soul continually pursue
The tender comfort that from thee I gain.
Thou art my sun, my stars, my very air,
Without thy grace, my world would be despair.


Mine eye, instructed by my yearning heart,
Doth gaze on thee with unremitting quest;
But what it sees, plays but a fleeting part,
And cannot give my troubled spirit rest.
For outward shows, though pleasing to the sight,
Are but the shadows of a deeper truth;
And beauty's bloom, however fresh and bright,
Is but the flower of transient, tender youth.
My soul seeks more than colour, form, and line,
It longs to know the essence of thy mind,
The inner grace, that makes thy being shine,
A treasure richer than the world can find.
So let my heart, not eye, thy worth proclaim,
And burn for thee with everlasting flame.


The golden sun, that gilds the morning's brow,
Doth climb its zenith, then begins to fade;
As swiftly doth our fleeting pleasure bow,
And vanish swift, as though it were unmade.
The blossoms fair, that greet the infant spring,
With tender hues and fragrance soft and sweet,
Are soon consumed by summer's scorching sting,
And cast as dust beneath our careless feet.
So too the joys that light our mortal span,
A momentary gleam, a fragile fire;
They mock our hopes, and flee before our plan,
And leave us longing with a vain desire.
Then seize the day, and hold each moment fast,
For fleeting joys are never made to last.


My cruel mistress, with her icy heart,
Doth scorn the ardour that within me glows;
She plays a cold and an unfeeling part,
And on my tender pleadings mercy shows
No jot, no whisper, but a harsh disdain,
As though my love were but a tiresome plea;
She binds me fast with an unyielding chain,
Yet grants no freedom, nor doth set me free.
Like moth to flame, I'm drawn to her bright eye,
Though all I find is torment and deep pain;
I yearn for comfort, yet my hopes deny,
The gentle solace I would fain attain.
Oh, wretched plight, to love where love's denied,
And bear the pangs of pride and scorn allied.


Let others boast of titles and of fame,
Of glittering riches, or of lineage high;
I place my trust in truth's enduring name,
And in the love that beams from thy dear eye.
For outward show, though splendid to behold,
Is often false, and fades with passing years;
As stories rich, in ancient tales untold,
Prove empty comfort, yielding only tears.
But genuine affection, deep and true,
Doth scorn the trappings of a gilded age;
It finds its worth in what the heart can do,
And writes its glory on a nobler page.
So let them shine in borrowed, fleeting light,
Whilst our true love burns ever pure and bright.


The fear of absence gnaws upon my soul,
Lest cruel fate should part us, dear, too soon;
And Time, that steals away beyond control,
Should snatch thy beauty 'neath a waning moon.
For every moment that I spend with thee,
A subtle dread doth shadow all my joy;
That such perfection should so transient be,
And earthly blisses time will swift destroy.
My heart doth ache, to think of future years,
When thy bright form might vanish from my sight,
And leave me drowned in melancholy tears,
To grope through darkness, yearning for thy light.
Then hold me fast, and banish all my dread,
Lest living love should slumber with the dead.


When all the world doth fade, and life is done,
And earthly honours turn to dust and rust,
When memory's thread is broken, one by one,
And mortal frames return to common dust;
Then shall my verse, if it hath any might,
Preserve thy image from the silent tomb,
And hold thy beauty 'gainst oblivion's night,
To triumph o'er the shadows and the gloom.
For in these lines, thy spirit shall reside,
A deathless essence, pure and ever new,
Beyond the reach of fortune's ebbing tide,
A steadfast monument, forever true.
So read, and live, when I am passed away,
And know my love outlasts life's fleeting day.


When forty winters shall besiege thy brow,
